Thus, these studies contradict the 'little to no effect on LUTS' portion of the claim but are less relevant to the exact target population and do not address treatment withdrawal due to adverse events directly.
conclusion: The statement is overall more supported than contradicted. This is driven primarily by a large randomized-trial-based systematic review directly matching the intervention/comparator framework and reporting little to no effect on withdrawal, prostate-related events, and LUTS, whereas the contradictory studies are indirect observational evidence in hypogonadal populations showing LUTS improvement rather than harm.

claim: In adults undergoing elective primary or revision hip or knee replacement surgery, tranexamic acid is probably the most effective pharmacological intervention for preventing the need for allogeneic blood transfusion.
support_summary: The supporting literature is extensive and consistently shows that tranexamic acid (TXA) lowers blood loss and reduces allogeneic transfusion in primary and revision hip/knee arthroplasty versus placebo or no TXA, including very large observational data (7.7% vs 20.1% transfusion in >870,000 arthroplasties, PMID: 25116268), multiple randomized trials in knee and hip arthroplasty (e.g., PMID: 20640094, PMID: 25810643, PMID: 27595071, PMID: 28689785), and revision hip/knee studies (PMID: 20574802, PMID: 25015759, PMID: 27426220, PMID: 30180060, PMID: 33155563). Meta-analyses and reviews also support efficacy, with one arthroplasty-specific meta-analysis reporting a marked reduction in allogeneic transfusion (OR 0.16, PMID: 14601276), another broader orthopedic meta-analysis finding a stronger effect estimate for TXA than aprotinin in pooled placebo-controlled trials (OR 0.17 vs 0.43, PMID: 17065899), and broader surgical syntheses concluding TXA reliably reduces transfusion requirements (PMID: 22611164, PMID: 29337665). Across studies, safety signals for venous thromboembolism are generally not increased, strengthening the case for routine prophylactic use in arthroplasty (PMID: 25116268, PMID: 29249226, PMID: 29337665). However, most supportive studies establish that TXA is effective rather than directly proving it is the single most effective pharmacologic option, and some reviews are indirect or non-comparative. A few items are only tangential to elective arthroplasty (e.g., fracture surgery or general orthopedic reviews), so they support the biological/clinical effect of TXA but are less direct for the exact claim.
contradict_summary: One older meta-analysis in total hip arthroplasty concluded that, when agents were analyzed separately, only aprotinin reached significance and TXA remained inconclusive, especially suggesting benefit only for aprotinin in revision THA (PMID: 16950041); this is the main direct contradiction to the claim that TXA is probably the most effective pharmacologic intervention. The other contradicting item is not really efficacy evidence: it is a Dutch survey showing erythropoietin and retransfusion techniques were commonly used while TXA had not been widely implemented (PMID: 24276360), which reflects practice patterns rather than comparative effectiveness. There is therefore a discrepancy in the contradiction set, because PMID: 24276360 does not directly show TXA is less effective than alternatives. Overall, contradiction is limited in quantity and is driven mainly by an older synthesis that conflicts with the much larger later body of evidence.
conclusion: The statement is overall more supported. The evidence base is large and consistent that TXA reduces allogeneic transfusion in elective primary and revision hip/knee arthroplasty, and while direct head-to-head proof of 'most effective' versus every other pharmacologic option is limited, the single substantial contradictory older meta-analysis is outweighed by many later trials, reviews, and large observational studies favoring TXA.

claim: In adults undergoing elective primary or revision hip or knee replacement surgery, aprotinin and epsilon-aminocaproic acid may be less effective than tranexamic acid for preventing the need for allogeneic blood transfusion.
support_summary: Supportive evidence is led by a meta-analysis of orthopedic surgery trials showing tranexamic acid (TXA) had a larger transfusion-reduction effect than aprotinin, while epsilon-aminocaproic acid (EACA) was not efficacious; however, this analysis was not limited to hip/knee arthroplasty and was not a direct head-to-head comparison across all agents (PMID: 17065899). A general benefit-risk review also states TXA is the most effective antifibrinolytic studied in major surgery, including orthopedics, with EACA possibly having a role, but this is indirect for elective hip/knee arthroplasty specifically (PMID: 18302446). In total knee replacement, one retrospective study directly observed lower transfusion rates and fewer red-cell units with TXA than with EACA, though the EACA group was small (PMID: 18383968). Multiple additional studies and reviews consistently show TXA reduces blood loss and transfusion needs in hip and/or knee replacement versus no antifibrinolytic or placebo, supporting TXA efficacy but only indirectly the claim of superiority over aprotinin or EACA (PMID: 20640094, PMID: 21600028, PMID: 23267449, PMID: 25785617, PMID: 35793255). One item in the support set appears discrepant: a cost/benefit review ranked aprotinin above TXA overall and was not specific to hip/knee arthroplasty, so it does not truly support the claim (PMID: 8572828).
contradict_summary: Contradicting evidence includes a meta-analysis in total hip arthroplasty reporting that, when analyzed separately, only aprotinin significantly reduced blood loss and transfusion requirements, with TXA results considered inconclusive, especially highlighting benefit in revision THA (PMID: 16950041). A randomized double-blind study in elective primary THA found no significant differences between oral EACA and oral TXA for blood loss, transfusion rate, hemoglobin/hematocrit changes, drainage, complications, or functional outcomes, directly arguing against EACA being less effective than TXA in that setting (PMID: 33443925). These contradictions are limited because one addresses aprotinin only in THA/revision THA and the other addresses EACA only in oral dosing for primary THA, but both are directly relevant to parts of the claim.
conclusion: The statement is overall somewhat more supported than contradicted. The main reason is that broader orthopedic evidence and one knee-replacement comparison favor TXA over aprotinin and especially over EACA, but the support is partly indirect, while the contradictory evidence includes directly relevant studies showing aprotinin benefit in revision THA and EACA-TXA equivalence in primary THA.

claim: In adults undergoing elective primary or revision hip or knee replacement surgery, higher-dose tranexamic acid may rank better for preventing bleeding, and no clear evidence of increased deep vein thrombosis was found with higher doses.
support_summary: Direct dose-comparison evidence comes mainly from a large retrospective cohort showing that a higher single preoperative IV TXA dose (30 mg/kg vs 15 mg/kg) reduced transfusion requirements in both total hip and knee arthroplasty, with no difference in deep vein thrombosis or pulmonary embolism between dose groups (PMID: 28851694). Several additional studies support the broader pattern that TXA reduces perioperative blood loss or transfusion in primary hip or knee arthroplasty and did not detect increased thromboembolic events, including randomized or prospective comparisons against placebo/control or matched controls (PMID: 26235005, PMID: 27108821, PMID: 29594527, PMID: 31070570). A randomized knee arthroplasty trial also found similar DVT prevalence across intravenous TXA, topical TXA, and control groups, with no DVT signal in the intravenous group (PMID: 29930576). However, most of these supporting studies do not actually compare higher versus lower TXA doses, so they only indirectly support the specific claim that higher-dose TXA ranks better for bleeding prevention. There is also a duplication-like discrepancy, as PMID: 26235005 and PMID: 27108821 appear to report the same 62-patient primary knee replacement study.
contradict_summary: No contradicting evidence items were provided.
conclusion: The statement is overall more supported than contradicted. The strongest direct evidence is a large comparative study favoring higher-dose TXA for reduced transfusion without an observed increase in DVT (PMID: 28851694), while the remaining studies consistently support TXA's bleeding reduction and lack of clear thrombotic harm but are mostly indirect for the specific higher-dose question.

claim: In mechanically ventilated preterm neonates undergoing endotracheal suctioning, facilitated tucking or related touch-based comfort interventions probably reduce pain compared with standard care or no intervention.
support_summary: Direct randomized crossover studies consistently found that facilitated tucking during endotracheal suctioning lowers neonatal pain scores versus routine care/no tucking, including reductions in PIPP or NIPS scores and faster calming/recovery in ventilated preterm or very low birthweight infants (PMID: 15123970, PMID: 16410042, PMID: 24999148). Additional suctioning-specific trials suggest related touch/containment interventions such as gentle human touch, swaddling, and possibly prone positioning also improve pain or comfort outcomes, with facilitated tucking and swaddling emerging as the strongest options in one recent RCT (PMID: 32316790, PMID: 36670708, PMID: 41405846). Systematic reviews focused on suctioning pain report that among heterogeneous nonpharmacologic interventions, facilitated tucking has the clearest evidence for efficacy and safety during endotracheal suctioning in preterm infants (PMID: 36100551), while broader reviews/meta-analyses of neonatal procedural pain also support facilitated tucking for painful procedures in preterm infants more generally (PMID: 19915422, PMID: 22059204, PMID: 32222816). Some cited studies are only indirectly relevant because they concern other procedures such as heel stick, echocardiography, or chest tube removal rather than suctioning (PMID: 7745488, PMID: 30144169, PMID: 34387216). One apparent discrepancy within the support set is a suctioning trial reporting no significant between-group difference during the procedure itself, though it found benefits before/after suctioning and in recovery, making its support only partial (PMID: 32690406).
contradict_summary: One randomized crossover study during endotracheal suctioning found no significant benefit of facilitated tucking on some pain/stress-related outcomes, including time to return to low pain scores and oxygen saturation changes, although heart rate changes were reduced with the intervention (PMID: 24266524). This study therefore weakens, but does not fully overturn, the claim because its abstract does not show a clear absence of all analgesic effect and still reports physiologic benefit. Notably, this contradicting trial appears closely related in population and intervention to supportive suctioning-specific trials, suggesting some inconsistency across individual studies rather than a strong body of negative evidence.
conclusion: The statement is overall more supported than contradicted. Multiple direct suctioning-specific randomized crossover trials and a suctioning-focused systematic review indicate that facilitated tucking, and to a lesser extent related touch-based interventions, probably reduce pain in mechanically ventilated preterm neonates, while the single contradicting study shows only partial inconsistency rather than strong negative evidence.

claim: In mechanically ventilated preterm neonates undergoing endotracheal suctioning, familiar odour and white noise have little or no effect on pain compared with no intervention.
support_summary: One randomized controlled trial found that white noise did not improve pain-related cortical response, pain scores, or behavioral/physiological parameters during neonatal arterial blood sampling versus control, suggesting little analgesic benefit in that setting (PMID: 35891905). However, this study involved procedural pain during blood sampling rather than mechanically ventilated preterm neonates undergoing endotracheal suctioning, so its relevance to the claim is indirect. Overall, the supporting evidence is limited to white noise alone and does not address familiar odour in the target suctioning context.
contradict_summary: A randomized controlled trial in preterm infants reported that both mother's own milk odor and white noise lowered pain scores and improved physiologic measures compared with control during and after heel lance, directly contradicting the idea that these interventions have little or no effect, though the procedure was heel lance rather than endotracheal suctioning (PMID: 39681072). More directly relevant, a randomized controlled trial during endotracheal suctioning in preterm infants found that white noise and facilitated tucking reduced pain before suctioning, but there was no statistically significant difference between groups during the suctioning procedure itself; breast milk smell was not clearly superior during the procedure (PMID: 32690406). This creates an internal discrepancy: the abstract's detailed results partly align with the claim for pain during suctioning, even though the paper's practice implications emphasize benefit. Recurring themes are that white noise and maternal/breast-milk odor may help in some neonatal procedures or peri-procedural periods, but direct evidence for analgesia during endotracheal suctioning itself is weaker.
conclusion: The statement is overall somewhat more contradicted than supported, because randomized trials suggest white noise and maternal/breast-milk odor can reduce pain in preterm infants compared with control, including one trial showing benefit in heel lance and another showing some benefit around endotracheal suctioning. However, the most procedure-specific abstract also reports no significant reduction in pain during suctioning itself, so the evidence is mixed rather than decisively contradictory.

claim: In mechanically ventilated neonates undergoing endotracheal suctioning, expressed breast milk and oral sucrose appear to offer no clear advantage over each other for reducing pain.
support_summary: One randomized controlled trial in preterm neonates on assisted ventilation found that endotracheal suctioning significantly increased pain scores and that expressed breast milk, oral sucrose, and swaddling did not differ in their ability to relieve this pain, directly supporting no clear advantage of breast milk over sucrose (PMID: 29123341). The abstract specifically reports no difference between expressed breast milk and sucrose in pain relief during suctioning. No clear discrepancy is apparent between the abstract and its categorization as supporting evidence.
contradict_summary: No contradicting evidence items were provided.
conclusion: The statement is overall more supported, because the available evidence includes a randomized controlled trial directly comparing expressed breast milk and oral sucrose during suctioning in ventilated preterm neonates and finding no difference between them (PMID: 29123341). However, confidence is moderate rather than strong because support rests on a single study and no additional confirming studies are provided.

claim: In adults with hepatorenal syndrome, transjugular intrahepatic portosystemic shunt placement may improve kidney function compared with conventional treatment, but the certainty of evidence is low.
support_summary: The supporting literature consistently suggests that TIPS may improve renal function in hepatorenal syndrome, with reported improvements in serum creatinine, creatinine clearance, sodium excretion, urine volume, and diuresis after shunt placement, including a small 18-patient series with significant renal functional improvement (PMID: 14696397) and several early case reports describing marked recovery of kidney function after TIPS (PMID: 7611221, PMID: 7797835, PMID: 8847056). A systematic review/meta-analysis of 9 publications (128 patients) found pooled renal function improvement rates of 93% in type 1 HRS and 83% overall, with significant post-TIPS improvement in several renal markers, but characterized the evidence as limited and noted frequent hepatic encephalopathy (PMID: 29422242). Multiple reviews and overviews describe TIPS as a promising or potentially effective bridge to transplantation for HRS, while explicitly emphasizing that evidence comes mainly from small series, noncomparative data, or limited numbers of studies and that the definitive role remains uncertain (PMID: 12118394, PMID: 17033432, PMID: 9308128, PMID: 11391027, PMID: 20581246). Some review articles discuss benefits mainly in refractory ascites or portal hypertension rather than direct comparative evidence in HRS, so they support the claim only indirectly and underscore the low-certainty nature of the evidence (PMID: 25438287, PMID: 31563220, PMID: 34131658, PMID: 37680416). Overall, the abstracts support possible kidney benefit from TIPS, but direct comparison against conventional treatment in adults with HRS is sparse and the evidence base is largely small and heterogeneous.
contradict_summary: There are no contradicting evidence items provided. Within the supportive set, however, several reviews explicitly note uncertainty about the definitive role of TIPS and the need for larger prospective studies, which tempers confidence rather than directly contradicting benefit (PMID: 12118394, PMID: 17033432, PMID: 9308128).
conclusion: The statement is overall more supported. The available abstracts indicate that TIPS may improve kidney function in adults with hepatorenal syndrome, but the evidence is mostly from small case series, case reports, and limited pooled observational data rather than robust comparative trials, making the certainty low.

claim: In adults with hepatorenal syndrome, transjugular intrahepatic portosystemic shunt placement may have little or uncertain effect on all-cause mortality compared with conventional treatment.
support_summary: Four review-style articles describe the role of TIPS in hepatorenal syndrome as uncertain or not yet established, emphasizing the lack of large prospective studies and unclear effects on survival rather than demonstrating a mortality benefit versus conventional treatment (PMID: 12118394, PMID: 17033432, PMID: 18417040). One review notes that for hepatorenal syndrome, evidence comes mainly from small series and mortality remains high because of severe underlying liver disease, despite possible physiologic benefits (PMID: 17033432). Another general TIPS review mentions hepatorenal syndrome as a novel indication but does not provide direct comparative mortality data, so its placement in support is indirect and somewhat weak for the specific mortality claim (PMID: 12530946). Overall, the recurring theme is uncertainty about whether TIPS improves survival in adults with hepatorenal syndrome.
contradict_summary: The main contradicting item is a systematic review/meta-analysis reporting pooled short-term and 1-year survival rates after TIPS and concluding that limited evidence suggests a potential survival benefit, although encephalopathy is common; however, the abstract does not clearly show a direct comparison against conventional treatment, so contradiction is only partial (PMID: 29422242). A small uncontrolled series in type-2 hepatorenal syndrome found improved renal parameters and ascites control after TIPS and framed TIPS as an attractive bridge to transplantation, but it did not directly report comparative all-cause mortality, making its contradiction indirect (PMID: 14696397). A single case report described resolution of hepatorenal syndrome after TIPS, which suggests possible benefit but is very weak evidence for mortality effects or treatment comparison (PMID: 7797835). Thus, the contradicting evidence mainly points to possible benefit signals, but much of it is non-comparative and limited.
conclusion: The evidence is overall unclear, with a slight lean toward support for the statement that TIPS has little or uncertain effect on all-cause mortality compared with conventional treatment. Reviews consistently describe the role of TIPS in hepatorenal syndrome as uncertain, while the opposing evidence suggests possible survival benefit but is largely based on limited, non-comparative, or lower-strength data.

claim: In adults with hepatorenal syndrome, transjugular intrahepatic portosystemic shunt placement may have little or uncertain effect on serious adverse events, overall morbidity, liver transplantation rates, and length of hospital stay compared with conventional treatment.
support_summary: Supportive evidence is mostly indirect and centers on uncertainty rather than demonstrated equivalence versus conventional treatment. Several reviews state that the role of TIPS in hepatorenal syndrome remains unclear or insufficiently studied, with calls for larger prospective studies and difficulty applying TIPS especially in type 1 HRS (PMID: 12118394, PMID: 18417040, PMID: 32284909, PMID: 7598487). One randomized trial in refractory ascites rather than established HRS found no survival or transplantation advantage with TIPS, while severe encephalopathy increased, making it only indirectly relevant and somewhat mixed for this claim (PMID: 12454841). The most directly relevant comparative HRS study was a recent propensity-matched inpatient analysis showing similar in-hospital mortality, hepatic encephalopathy, and liver transplantation rates between TIPS and no TIPS, but longer hospital stay with TIPS (PMID: 40495002). Overall, the supporting set is dominated by narrative reviews and indirect evidence, with only limited direct comparative data on the specific outcomes in HRS.
contradict_summary: Contradicting evidence mainly suggests potential benefit of TIPS on renal function, ascites control, or survival, but much of it is low-level or indirect. A small uncontrolled series in type 2 HRS reported improved renal parameters and ascites after TIPS with few complications, proposing TIPS as a bridge to transplantation, but it lacked a conventional-treatment comparator (PMID: 14696397). Case reports and narrative reviews similarly describe HRS resolution or improved renal function and possible improved transplant readiness/survival, yet these do not directly test the claim's comparative outcomes (PMID: 7797835, PMID: 8847056, PMID: 18417039, PMID: 30087522). A systematic review/meta-analysis found improved renal indices and possible survival benefit after TIPS, but also a high hepatic encephalopathy rate and no direct controlled comparison against conventional treatment in the abstract (PMID: 29422242). Some items appear miscategorized because they discuss recurrent ascites or portal-hypertension complications more broadly rather than comparative HRS outcomes specifically (PMID: 30550458, PMID: 34131658, PMID: 40636403).
conclusion: The evidence is unclear, with a slight lean toward the claim because the most direct comparative HRS evidence shows similar short-term mortality, encephalopathy, and transplantation rates but longer hospital stay with TIPS (PMID: 40495002), while many other sources explicitly describe the role of TIPS in HRS as uncertain. However, contradictory studies suggest possible improvements in renal function and prognosis, although these are often uncontrolled, indirect, or not directly comparable with conventional treatment.

claim: In preterm infants with or at risk of respiratory distress syndrome, rescue laryngeal mask airway surfactant administration may reduce the need for mechanical ventilation compared with surfactant administration via endotracheal tube using an intubate-surfactant-extubate approach.
support_summary: Direct support comes primarily from one randomized trial comparing LMA surfactant delivery with endotracheal intubation-surfactant-rapid extubation, in which treatment failure to avoid mechanical ventilation was markedly lower with LMA (30% vs 77%), largely due to fewer early failures (PMID: 26633145). Another multicenter randomized trial found LMA surfactant reduced intubation/mechanical ventilation versus CPAP alone (38% vs 64%), supporting LMA feasibility and benefit, though not directly against INSURE (PMID: 29174079), and a case report also describes successful LMA rescue surfactant without subsequent intubation (PMID: 23917771). A systematic review/meta-analysis of six RCTs concluded LMA may be an effective alternative surfactant delivery route, but the abstract provides only limited direct quantitative detail for mechanical ventilation outcomes and calls for larger trials (PMID: 33517565). Many additional supportive items are reviews or studies of other less-invasive techniques such as LISA/MIST rather than LMA specifically; these consistently report lower mechanical ventilation than INSURE/intubation (e.g., PMID: 40008543, PMID: 31424177, PMID: 36053649, PMID: 38503270, PMID: 41035245), but this is indirect evidence for the specific LMA claim and should be interpreted as a broader theme favoring less-invasive surfactant delivery rather than direct proof for LMA.
contradict_summary: Contradicting evidence is limited and mostly indirect. Two small retrospective studies found no significant difference in mechanical ventilation within 72 hours between LISA and INSURE, concluding the approaches were similarly effective (PMID: 38566872; PMID: 39526720), but these evaluate LISA rather than LMA and therefore only indirectly challenge the claim. An older Cochrane review found only one small trial of rescue LMA surfactant versus no treatment and reported no significant difference in subsequent mechanical ventilation; however, this was underpowered and did not directly compare LMA with INSURE/endotracheal surfactant (PMID: 21735428). Overall, the contradictory set is weakened by small sample sizes, indirect comparators, and lack of a strong direct LMA-versus-INSURE negative study.
conclusion: The statement is overall more supported than contradicted. The strongest evidence is a direct randomized comparison showing lower mechanical ventilation with LMA versus endotracheal intubation-surfactant-extubation-type management (PMID: 26633145), while the opposing evidence is mostly small, indirect, or underpowered and often concerns LISA rather than LMA specifically.

claim: In preterm infants with or at risk of respiratory distress syndrome, laryngeal mask airway surfactant administration probably reduces the need for mechanical ventilation compared with no surfactant administration while showing little or no difference in death or bronchopulmonary dysplasia.
support_summary: Direct evidence for the claim comes mainly from a multicenter randomized controlled trial showing that surfactant delivered via laryngeal mask airway (LMA) in preterm infants with moderate RDS on CPAP significantly reduced intubation/mechanical ventilation versus CPAP without surfactant (38% vs 64%) and caused no serious adverse events (PMID: 29174079). A case report also described successful LMA surfactant delivery without subsequent intubation, but this is very low-level evidence (PMID: 23917771). Several reviews and broader less-invasive surfactant administration (LISA/MIST) syntheses consistently report reduced need for mechanical ventilation or intubation with less-invasive surfactant approaches, and they often note uncertain or limited effects on BPD/death, though these data are indirect because they are not specific to LMA-versus-no-surfactant comparisons (PMID: 29042870, PMID: 35110053, PMID: 36539205, PMID: 40736358, PMID: 41597158). Some supporting items are only loosely relevant because they concern surfactant generally or thin-catheter LISA rather than LMA specifically, creating an evidence-discrepancy within the support set (PMID: 11359038, PMID: 28540346, PMID: 34515188, PMID: 36053649).
contradict_summary: The lone contradicting study is an older randomized trial of intratracheal human surfactant versus mechanical ventilation alone, which found surfactant reduced death or bronchopulmonary dysplasia and improved respiratory outcomes (PMID: 3889259). This conflicts with the claim's statement of little or no difference in death/BPD, but it is not a direct test of LMA surfactant administration because the intervention was intratracheal surfactant in infants with severe RDS. Thus, the contradiction is biologically relevant to surfactant efficacy overall but only indirectly applicable to the specific LMA-versus-no-surfactant claim.
conclusion: The statement is overall more supported than contradicted. The strongest directly relevant evidence is an RCT showing LMA surfactant lowers need for intubation/mechanical ventilation versus no surfactant, while evidence about death or BPD is mostly indirect and suggests uncertainty or little clear difference for LMA specifically rather than a proven benefit.

claim: In preterm infants with or at risk of respiratory distress syndrome, laryngeal mask airway surfactant administration appears to have little or no effect on death or bronchopulmonary dysplasia compared with surfactant administration via endotracheal tube or no surfactant administration, and evidence is insufficient to support routine use in extremely preterm infants.
support_summary: The only item in the support group is not directly about laryngeal mask airway (LMA) surfactant administration but about nebulized surfactant, and it found only one small trial with no significant difference in chronic lung disease or other outcomes versus no treatment, concluding that evidence was insufficient for clinical use (PMID: 23076945). This provides only indirect support for the claim’s theme of insufficient evidence and lack of clear benefit, but it does not specifically address LMA, death, or bronchopulmonary dysplasia (BPD).
contradict_summary: Several contradicting items concern less invasive surfactant administration broadly, especially thin-catheter LISA/MIST rather than LMA, and therefore are only indirect contradictions to this LMA-specific claim. A meta-analysis of 18 RCTs found LISA reduced the composite of death or BPD compared with INSURE or mechanical ventilation (PMID: 35379135), and a narrative review argued the evidence base justifies routine LISA use in neonatal units (PMID: 35986148); however, these do not evaluate LMA specifically. A broader review reported that non-invasive surfactant approaches are associated with less mechanical ventilation and lower BPD overall, but it explicitly noted that LMA-assisted delivery shows promise while remaining limited by heterogeneity and applicability constraints (PMID: 41597158). The most directly relevant study, an LMA systematic review/meta-analysis of 6 RCTs, concluded LMA might be an effective alternative but that its effectiveness is uncertain and further high-quality trials, especially in extremely preterm infants, are needed (PMID: 33517565), which only partially contradicts the claim and also aligns with the claim’s caution about insufficient evidence for routine use in extremely preterm infants.
conclusion: The evidence is overall unclear but leans slightly toward the claim because the directly relevant LMA-specific review does not show firm evidence of benefit on death or BPD and explicitly calls for more data, especially in extremely preterm infants (PMID: 33517565). Much of the apparent contradiction comes from studies of other less invasive surfactant techniques such as LISA/MIST rather than LMA, limiting their relevance to this specific statement.

claim: For adults considering treatment or screening decisions, patient decision aids compared with usual care probably increase the likelihood that decisions are informed and consistent with personal values.
support_summary: Multiple large systematic reviews and meta-analyses of randomized trials consistently found that patient decision aids improve knowledge, accurate risk perceptions, feeling informed, and clarity about personal values versus usual care, with several reviews also finding greater values-congruent or informed value-based choices, though effect sizes vary and value-congruence evidence is often lower certainty than knowledge outcomes (PMID: 17873255, PMID: 19588325, PMID: 21975733, PMID: 24470076, PMID: 28402085). The most directly relevant evidence includes pooled estimates showing improved informed values-based choice or values-choice congruence, including explicit values clarification components increasing values-congruent choices (PMID: 21975733, PMID: 24470076, PMID: 28402085). Individual trials in screening and treatment contexts also report improved informed choice, knowledge, reduced decisional conflict, and better preparation for decision-making, such as bowel cancer screening and orthopedic care studies (PMID: 20978060, PMID: 28763411, PMID: 37815283). More recent focused reviews in specific conditions generally echo these findings, especially for knowledge and feeling informed/clearer about values, although some are narrower or lower certainty (PMID: 39954994, PMID: 34302958, PMID: 39077149). Many additional supportive citations are development, usability, protocol, or implementation papers rather than comparative effectiveness studies, so they add context but are less probative for the claim.
contradict_summary: Contradictory evidence is limited and mostly shows that benefits are not universal rather than that decision aids are ineffective overall. In a prostate cancer treatment cluster RCT, an online decision aid did not improve objective knowledge, involvement, or decisional conflict versus standard counseling and slightly worsened satisfaction with information/preparation in some users (PMID: 29752528). In prostate screening, decision aids appeared to amplify baseline negative attitudes, reducing screening among men already concerned about screening limitations; however, this reflects changed choices rather than clearly worse decision quality or poorer values-concordance, so its contradiction to the claim is indirect (PMID: 25724634). A small mixed-methods feasibility study in advanced Parkinson disease found improved knowledge but no improvement in shared decision-making because the intervention was not used as intended, suggesting implementation challenges more than clear failure of informed, values-consistent decision-making (PMID: 40080221).
conclusion: Overall, the statement is more supported than contradicted. The evidence base includes several large systematic reviews of randomized trials showing consistent improvements in knowledge, feeling informed, clarity about values, and some evidence of more values-congruent choices, whereas the contradictory studies are fewer, narrower, and often reflect context-specific limitations or implementation issues rather than a broad reversal of effect.

claim: For adults considering treatment or screening decisions, patient decision aids compared with usual care improve knowledge, accuracy of risk perceptions, feeling informed, clarity about personal values, and active participation in decision-making.
support_summary: The strongest supporting evidence comes from multiple large Cochrane systematic reviews of randomized trials showing that, versus usual care, patient decision aids consistently increase knowledge, improve accurate risk perceptions when probabilities are included, reduce decisional conflict related to feeling uninformed and unclear about values, and reduce passive decision-making (PMID: 19588325, PMID: 21975733, PMID: 24470076, PMID: 28402085). These reviews also report improved values-congruent choices and greater involvement in decisions across many treatment and screening contexts, with high- or moderate-quality evidence for several core outcomes (PMID: 24470076, PMID: 28402085, PMID: 28810006). More focused reviews and trials in older adults, cancer, cardiovascular care, colorectal screening, atrial fibrillation, and diabetes generally echo these findings, especially for knowledge, feeling informed, values clarity, and participation, though some domains are not measured in every study (PMID: 27098100, PMID: 30652610, PMID: 40440359, PMID: 27593418, PMID: 39788611, PMID: 39954994). Individual RCTs and pilot RCTs also report improvements in knowledge, decisional conflict, values clarity, risk perception, or participation versus control/usual care in specific decisions such as breech presentation, bowel screening, cervical screening, and stroke prevention (PMID: 17217360, PMID: 20978060, PMID: 36482142, PMID: 39788611). Some items in the support set are weaker or only indirectly relevant because they are protocols, development studies, uncontrolled pre-post studies, or lack a usual-care comparator, which should be treated as limited support rather than confirmatory evidence (for example PMID: 20843369, PMID: 20849625, PMID: 31596249, PMID: 33397488).
contradict_summary: Contradictory evidence is limited and partial. In a randomized trial of a web-based sickle cell disease decision aid, participants showed some improvements in decisional self-efficacy and feeling informed, but the study could not demonstrate improved knowledge or involvement overall, with substantial missing data limiting conclusions (PMID: 31799940). A systematic review of digital decision-support tools for atrial fibrillation found reduced decisional conflict but little to no change in patient knowledge versus usual care, suggesting that digital formats in this narrower setting may not reproduce the broader knowledge gains seen in general decision-aid reviews (PMID: 38950915). These studies do not clearly refute the broader claim for patient decision aids overall, but they do indicate heterogeneity by condition, tool type, and study quality.
conclusion: Overall, the statement is strongly supported. Multiple large systematic reviews of randomized trials consistently show that patient decision aids improve knowledge, risk perception, feeling informed, values clarity, and participation compared with usual care, while the small amount of contradictory evidence is condition-specific, partly limited by missing data, and does not outweigh the broader evidence base.

claim: For adults considering treatment or screening decisions, patient decision aids compared with usual care do not reduce decision regret, and consultation length is not meaningfully shorter when the aids are used before a consultation or is slightly longer when used during a consultation.
support_summary: The supporting evidence mainly addresses consultation length rather than decision regret. A video-based study of anticoagulation consultations found computer-based decision aids prolonged visits compared with paper guidelines, especially for more explicit tools, suggesting in-consultation aids can take longer rather than shorten visits (PMID: 17214891). A systematic review of encounter decision aids reported that 9 of 13 trials found no prolongation of outpatient consultations, though the overall evidence was low quality (PMID: 30301874). A prospective cohort study in lung cancer diagnostics and breast cancer treatment similarly found consultations were only slightly, non-significantly longer with shared decision making and a patient decision aid (PMID: 33390303). None of these supporting studies directly show that decision aids do not reduce decision regret.
contradict_summary: Contradicting evidence is stronger on decision regret. A prospective cohort study in lung cancer diagnostics reported significantly lower decision regret with shared decision making plus a patient decision aid versus usual care (PMID: 31129012), and a multisite randomized trial in breast cancer surgery found a picture-based conversation aid lowered decision regret compared with usual care (PMID: 33170506). Some items in the contradict set are miscategorized or only indirectly relevant: a large Cochrane review actually aligns with the claim on consultation time by reporting a median 2.6-minute increase and does not directly address regret (PMID: 28402085), while a trapeziometacarpal arthritis trial focused on decisional conflict and did not report clear regret effects in the abstract (PMID: 30031600). A breast reconstruction trial compared two decision aids rather than decision aid versus usual care, so its finding that both groups had reduced regret is less directly informative for the claim (PMID: 36696307). Overall, the recurring contradiction theme is that at least some decision aids appear to reduce decision regret versus usual care.
conclusion: The statement is overall mixed but somewhat more contradicted, because the best direct evidence on decision regret includes studies showing lower regret with patient decision aids versus usual care, whereas the supporting evidence mostly addresses consultation length and not regret. The consultation-length part of the claim is reasonably supported by several studies, but the regret component is not.

claim: In adults and children with biopsy-proven IgA nephropathy, renin-angiotensin system inhibition probably reduces proteinuria compared with placebo or no treatment, with little or no clear effect on kidney failure, doubling of serum creatinine, or complete remission of proteinuria.
support_summary: Several randomized or placebo-controlled studies in biopsy-proven IgA nephropathy report that renin-angiotensin system inhibition lowers proteinuria, including fosinopril versus placebo with stable creatinine clearance (PMID: 8052432), trandolapril or candesartan versus placebo over 3 months (PMID: 11092994), and aliskiren versus placebo as add-on therapy with significant proteinuria reduction but only modest eGFR change (PMID: 23675422). Additional small trials and observational studies suggest ACE inhibitors/ARBs, alone or in combination, produce substantial antiproteinuric effects, though many lack a placebo/no-treatment comparator and therefore only indirectly support the claim (PMID: 10213639, PMID: 7593601, PMID: 18051227). Meta-analytic and review evidence also consistently emphasizes reduction in proteinuria with ACEI/ARB treatment in IgA nephropathy, while noting that longer-term renal outcome data remain limited or controversial (PMID: 31700813, PMID: 29989013, PMID: 33594462). Across these abstracts, clear benefits on kidney failure, doubling of serum creatinine, or complete remission are not firmly demonstrated, and several papers explicitly say long-term renal prognosis remains uncertain (PMID: 8052432, PMID: 31700813). Some listed supporting items are narrative reviews or broad kidney-disease discussions rather than direct comparative IgA nephropathy trials, which weakens their evidentiary weight and creates a support-quality discrepancy (PMID: 19773725, PMID: 20441574, PMID: 24641955, PMID: 28033590, PMID: 37022667).
contradict_summary: The only contradicting item is a retrospective single-center cohort that found no significant difference in proteinuria reduction after 6 months and suggested kidney function was better preserved when prednisone was added to RAS blockade than with RAS blockers alone (PMID: 21663387). However, this study did not compare RAS inhibition with placebo or no treatment, so it does not directly refute the claim that RAS inhibition reduces proteinuria versus no treatment. Its main signal is that RAS blockade alone may be less effective than combination therapy for renal preservation, not that it is ineffective overall. Thus, the contradiction is limited and indirect.
conclusion: Overall, the statement is more supported than contradicted. The strongest evidence from placebo-controlled trials and meta-analysis supports a reduction in proteinuria with RAS inhibition in IgA nephropathy, while evidence for effects on kidney failure, serum creatinine doubling, or complete remission remains sparse or inconclusive.

claim: In adults and children with biopsy-proven IgA nephropathy, renin-angiotensin system inhibition probably reduces proteinuria and improves kidney function compared with symptomatic treatment, while the effect on kidney failure remains uncertain.
support_summary: Supporting evidence is led by randomized and comparative studies showing antiproteinuric effects of renin-angiotensin system (RAS) blockade in biopsy-proven IgA nephropathy. A placebo-controlled crossover RCT found fosinopril significantly reduced proteinuria versus placebo in normotensive adults, while creatinine clearance remained unchanged and long-term renal prognosis was unresolved (PMID: 8052432). Another randomized crossover trial showed add-on aliskiren significantly lowered proteinuria versus placebo in patients already on ACE inhibitor/ARB therapy, although eGFR fell modestly over 16 weeks, so kidney-function benefit was not clearly demonstrated in that study (PMID: 23675422). A Bayesian network meta-analysis of 17 RCTs reported that ACEI/ARB combinations had the greatest probability of reducing proteinuria and that ACEIs appeared most renoprotective for kidney function preservation (PMID: 34268008). Several reviews and guideline-style papers describe RAS inhibition as standard or cornerstone supportive therapy for proteinuric/hypertensive IgA nephropathy in adults and children, but these are indirect and not direct comparisons with symptomatic treatment; some recent support items are reviews, case reports, or combination-therapy studies rather than clean tests of RAS blockade alone (PMID: 33594462, PMID: 39188719, PMID: 41587026).
contradict_summary: Only one contradicting study was provided, and it offers limited contradiction. In a retrospective single-center cohort, RAS blockers alone did not show a significant proteinuria reduction after 6 months, and kidney function was less well preserved than with RAS blockers plus prednisone; however, this compares RAS monotherapy with combination therapy, not with symptomatic treatment, so it does not directly refute benefit versus symptomatic care (PMID: 21663387). The same study therefore mainly suggests possible added benefit from prednisone rather than clear ineffectiveness of RAS blockade itself.
conclusion: Overall, the claim is more supported than contradicted. The strongest evidence consistently supports reduction in proteinuria with RAS inhibition, and there is some evidence or expert synthesis suggesting kidney-function preservation, while direct evidence on progression to kidney failure remains limited or uncertain.

claim: In adults and children with biopsy-proven IgA nephropathy, tonsillectomy added to standard care may increase remission of proteinuria and microscopic haematuria and may reduce relapse of proteinuria and haematuria, but the evidence is low certainty and appears to come from Japanese patients only.
support_summary: Support comes mainly from one randomized single-center trial showing that, in 98 patients with biopsy-proven IgA nephropathy, tonsillectomy plus drug therapy led to faster remission, higher cumulative remission rates, longer remission duration, and lower relapse rates for both hematuria and proteinuria versus drug therapy alone over 4 years (PMID: 26727697). A narrative review reports observational studies, largely from Japan, in which tonsillectomy was associated with fewer episodes of macrohematuria and less microhematuria/proteinuria, and it emphasizes that the evidence is retrospective, short-term, and vulnerable to confounding (PMID: 22802576). A small case report in recurrent IgA nephropathy after renal transplantation described disappearance of proteinuria after tonsillectomy plus steroid pulse therapy, but this is indirect to the claim because it involves renal allograft recurrence and only two patients (PMID: 20040355). Overall, the abstracts support possible benefit for remission and relapse outcomes, while also indicating that much of the evidence base is limited and appears heavily derived from Japanese practice settings (PMID: 22802576).
contradict_summary: No direct contradicting evidence items were provided. There is, however, some internal limitation within the supporting set: the review notes that available studies were short-term and retrospective with confounding concerns, and the case report is only indirect evidence rather than a contradiction (PMID: 22802576; PMID: 20040355).
conclusion: The statement is overall more supported than contradicted. The strongest abstract is a randomized trial showing improved remission and lower relapse with tonsillectomy (PMID: 26727697), while the review and case report are consistent with benefit but also underscore that the broader evidence base is limited, low-certainty, and largely from Japanese experience.

claim: In adults with dry eye disease caused by meibomian gland dysfunction, LipiFlow has similar effects to basic warm compresses on dry eye signs and symptoms.
support_summary: A Cochrane systematic review of 13 randomized trials found that in five trials directly comparing LipiFlow with basic warm compresses, symptom results at 4 weeks were conflicting, but there was no evidence of a difference in meibomian gland expression, meibum quality, or tear breakup time; the review concluded LipiFlow performs similarly to other dry eye treatments, although certainty was low or very low and many trials had high risk of bias (PMID: 39562261). A 15-year narrative review also states that some studies found daily eyelid hygiene, warm compresses, and/or massage produced similar benefit to a single LipiFlow treatment, though this review mainly emphasizes overall LipiFlow effectiveness rather than direct equivalence and is therefore only indirect support for the claim (PMID: 38879718). Overall, the supporting evidence centers on direct comparative trials and review-level synthesis suggesting little clear difference versus basic warm compresses, but with substantial limitations in evidence quality.
contradict_summary: One prospective randomized trial in cataract patients with mild-moderate meibomian gland dysfunction found that preoperative LipiFlow significantly improved noninvasive breakup time, SPEED symptoms, and meibomian gland function before surgery and maintained better postoperative ocular surface status than warm compresses plus lid massage; changes from baseline differed significantly between groups, favoring LipiFlow (PMID: 36729441). However, this study is in a peri-cataract surgery setting focused on prevention of postoperative dry eye, which is narrower than the general claim about adults with dry eye disease caused by meibomian gland dysfunction.
conclusion: The statement is overall more supported than contradicted. The strongest evidence is a systematic review directly comparing LipiFlow with basic warm compresses and finding no clear differences in several signs, although confidence is limited by low-certainty, biased trials; the main contradictory study suggests LipiFlow may outperform warm compresses in a specific pre-cataract surgery context rather than the broader MGD-related dry eye population.

claim: In adults with dry eye disease caused by meibomian gland dysfunction, LipiFlow has similar effects to other commonly used treatments, including thermostatic devices, eyelid hygiene plus LipiFlow versus eyelid hygiene alone, topical lifitegrast, and oral doxycycline, on most dry eye signs and symptoms.
support_summary: A 2024 Cochrane review of 13 randomized trials found that LipiFlow generally performed similarly to other dry eye/meibomian gland dysfunction treatments, with no clear differences versus basic warm compresses for meibomian gland expression, meibum quality, or tear breakup time, and low-certainty evidence overall; thermostatic devices showed a modest OSDI advantage at 4 weeks, but the authors still concluded LipiFlow performs similarly to other DED treatments (PMID: 39562261). A 15-year review of 55 LipiFlow studies also reported that some studies found daily eyelid hygiene, warm compresses, and/or massage had benefits similar to LipiFlow, though this was a broad narrative review rather than a focused head-to-head comparison of all treatments named in the claim (PMID: 38879718). Notably, neither supporting abstract directly addresses comparisons with topical lifitegrast or oral doxycycline, so support for those parts of the claim is indirect or absent from these abstracts.
contradict_summary: A randomized controlled trial comparing TearCare with LipiFlow found that TearCare improved symptoms and, in patients with more severe gland dysfunction, performed significantly better than LipiFlow on some symptom measures, which argues against strict equivalence with at least one thermostatic-type device in some subgroups (PMID: 36065356). An American Academy of Ophthalmology report reviewing 11 LipiFlow studies found that most studies reported greater efficacy for thermal pulsation than standard warm compress therapy and eyelid hygiene, although two independent level I studies found no significant difference, making this evidence mixed rather than uniformly contradictory (PMID: 37642619). This contradicting set therefore mainly suggests that LipiFlow may sometimes be superior to conventional hygiene/warm compress therapy, and sometimes inferior to another device in selected subgroups, rather than consistently 'similar' across all comparators. Neither contradicting abstract directly evaluates topical lifitegrast or oral doxycycline.
conclusion: The evidence is mixed but leans slightly toward the claim being supported for some comparators, especially warm compresses/eyelid hygiene and possibly thermostatic devices, because a Cochrane review concluded LipiFlow performs similarly overall despite low-certainty evidence. However, the claim is broader than the abstracts support, since direct abstract-level evidence for topical lifitegrast and oral doxycycline is lacking here, and some studies suggest meaningful differences rather than equivalence.

claim: In adults with dry eye disease caused by meibomian gland dysfunction, no intervention-related vision-threatening adverse events were reported with LipiFlow in the included trials.
support_summary: Multiple reviews and trial reports consistently describe LipiFlow as having a favorable safety profile in adults with meibomian gland dysfunction-related dry eye, with no reported vision-threatening intervention-related adverse events in the included studies. A Cochrane systematic review of 13 randomized trials explicitly states that no trial reported any intervention-related, vision-threatening adverse events (PMID: 39562261). A 15-year comprehensive review similarly reports that across 55 studies, all LipiFlow-related adverse events were transient, non-vision-threatening, required no treatment, and no serious adverse events were reported (PMID: 38879718). Individual randomized trials and meta-analyses further support low adverse-event rates, describing only non-serious or comparable adverse events versus controls, although several of these abstracts do not explicitly mention “vision-threatening” events and therefore provide only indirect support (PMID: 22222996, PMID: 34374808, PMID: 35383732, PMID: 35192836). No major discrepancy is evident within the supporting set, though the strength of support varies depending on whether the abstract directly addresses vision-threatening events.
contradict_summary: No contradicting evidence items were provided.
conclusion: The statement is overall strongly supported. The strongest evidence comes from systematic reviews, especially the Cochrane review and the 15-year safety review, both of which directly report no intervention-related vision-threatening adverse events with LipiFlow in the included trials/studies.

claim: In people with gallstone disease, modifying dietary fat intake has insufficient evidence to show benefit over a standard diet for important clinical outcomes.
support_summary: One review of epidemiologic studies found no convincing evidence that total fat or cholesterol intake plays a role in gallbladder disease risk and concluded that specific dietary recommendations could not be made, which supports the claim of insufficient evidence for benefit from fat modification (PMID: 10447244). However, this review addresses disease risk/prevention rather than treatment outcomes among people who already have gallstone disease, so its relevance to the claim’s population and clinical outcomes is indirect.
contradict_summary: Two studies suggest possible benefit from specific fatty acid patterns, but both mainly address risk/incidence rather than outcomes in patients with established gallstone disease. A large prospective cohort in men found higher monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fat intake was associated with lower incidence of symptomatic gallstone disease over 14 years (PMID: 15466768). A cross-sectional study with Mendelian randomization reported that arachidonic acid-related intake/plasma lipids were associated with reduced gallstone risk and suggested preventive potential (PMID: 39865707). This creates a scope discrepancy: these studies contradict the claim only indirectly because they concern prevention or association, not randomized comparisons of fat-modified versus standard diets for important clinical outcomes in people with existing gallstone disease.
conclusion: The evidence is overall unclear but leans toward the claim being supported in its specific clinical context. Although some observational and genetic evidence suggests certain unsaturated fats may reduce gallstone risk, the available abstracts do not directly show that altering dietary fat improves important clinical outcomes over a standard diet in people who already have gallstone disease.

claim: In adults with gallstones receiving ursodeoxycholic acid, a low-cholesterol diet did not show a meaningful effect on gallstone dissolution compared with a standard diet.
support_summary: One randomized study in patients with radiolucent gallstones treated with ursodeoxycholic acid compared a standard diet with several dietary modifications, including a low-cholesterol diet, and found that the low-cholesterol diet did not significantly change UDCA-induced effects on bile acid pools, bile acid synthesis, biliary lipid secretion, or cholesterol saturation versus the standard diet (PMID: 3004189). The abstract does not directly report gallstone dissolution outcomes, but its physiological findings are consistent with no meaningful added benefit of a low-cholesterol diet during UDCA therapy (PMID: 3004189). Overall, the available supporting evidence is limited to this single mechanistic trial.
contradict_summary: No contradicting evidence items were provided.
conclusion: The statement is overall more supported than contradicted because the only cited study found no significant additional effect of a low-cholesterol diet versus a standard diet during UDCA treatment (PMID: 3004189). However, confidence is moderate rather than strong because the abstract emphasizes bile chemistry and kinetics rather than directly reporting gallstone dissolution outcomes.

claim: In adults with gallstone disease, the available randomized trial evidence does not show serious adverse events from dietary fat modification, but the overall evidence base is too limited to establish effectiveness.
support_summary: No non-zero supporting evidence items were available.
contradict_summary: No non-zero contradicting evidence items were available.
conclusion: This statement had only zero-score or missing-score rows in the input file.

claim: In people with clinically suspected giant cell arteritis, temporal artery ultrasound halo sign has limited and heterogeneous diagnostic accuracy, with moderate sensitivity when specificity is high and generally good specificity.
support_summary: Multiple primary studies and meta-analyses support that the temporal artery ultrasound halo sign has only moderate and variable sensitivity, while specificity is usually good to high. Reported examples include sensitivity/specificity of 40%/79% or 40%/93% for thicker halos (PMID: 12186513), 72%/72% (PMID: 19646351), 40%/81% to 42%/94% (PMID: 23981759), and 10-17%/100% in one low-sensitivity series (PMID: 20810501); a recent retrospective study also found low halo-sign sensitivity with high specificity versus clinical diagnosis and biopsy (PMID: 39907604). Meta-analyses found pooled halo-sign sensitivity around 68-69% with specificity 81-91%, and higher specificity but lower sensitivity for bilateral halos, while explicitly noting significant between-study heterogeneity (PMID: 15738455, PMID: 20210989, PMID: 30408588). A narrative review also described very wide ranges across studies (sensitivity 9-100%, specificity 66-100%), reinforcing heterogeneity (PMID: 36262280). One older study reported higher sensitivity (86%) but only moderate specificity and poor positive predictive value, which still fits the theme of limited overall accuracy rather than a uniformly strong test (PMID: 12064840).
contradict_summary: Several contradicting reports portray ultrasound halo-based assessment as highly accurate, often from single centers, reviews, or protocols using expert operators, optimized equipment, or extended vascular assessments rather than isolated temporal halo sign performance. Some primary studies reported high sensitivity and specificity, such as 95.4%/91.3% (PMID: 15498914), 79%/100% (PMID: 22693039), 81%/98% (PMID: 27070887), 91.6%/95.8% for Doppler ultrasound overall (PMID: 28244857), and 94%/84% for multicentre vascular ultrasound including multiple arterial territories (PMID: 38287632); other studies of scoring systems or extended protocols also found high accuracy (PMID: 35118098, PMID: 37525060, PMID: 38343643). However, several items are reviews, opinion pieces, case reports, or broad statements lacking abstract-level quantitative evidence specific to the temporal artery halo sign alone (PMID: 10948760, PMID: 19153753, PMID: 28460064, PMID: 31327543, PMID: 33817861, PMID: 35278298), creating a discrepancy between their categorization and the more mixed quantitative literature. Even some partially contradicting evidence still reports sensitivity only in the moderate range with high specificity, e.g., 77.7%/100% (PMID: 18799059), 80%/80% (PMID: 35426608), and pooled 76%/93% in a 2023 meta-analysis (PMID: 36755336), which does not fully negate the claim of limited and heterogeneous accuracy. Overall, the contradicting set suggests that under expert or expanded-ultrasound conditions diagnostic performance can be substantially better than the more conservative summary in the claim.
conclusion: The statement is overall more supported. The strongest support comes from multiple meta-analyses and several primary studies showing moderate pooled sensitivity, generally good specificity, and marked between-study heterogeneity, whereas much of the contradicting evidence reflects expert-center, extended-protocol, or review-based settings that may overstate performance of the temporal halo sign alone.

claim: In people with clinically suspected giant cell arteritis, temporal artery ultrasound cannot currently be concluded to replace temporal artery biopsy for diagnosis because the comparative evidence is very uncertain.
support_summary: Several studies and reviews indicate that temporal artery ultrasound has variable and often insufficient diagnostic performance versus biopsy, with low-to-moderate sensitivity and imperfect specificity, arguing against concluding that it can replace biopsy in suspected giant cell arteritis; for example, one prospective study found ultrasound sensitivity 42.6% and specificity 65.7% versus biopsy sensitivity 73.7% and specificity 100% (PMID: 30318270), and an older diagnostic study found only 40% sensitivity for the halo sign (PMID: 12186513). Reviews also explicitly state that imaging, including Doppler ultrasound, cannot currently replace temporal artery biopsy and that biopsy remains the gold standard (PMID: 14673385), with conflicting literature and meta-analyses often favoring biopsy sensitivity while noting false-positive ultrasound findings and pathology's ability to detect mimics (PMID: 32193868). A systematic review/meta-analysis reported only moderate pooled accuracy for the halo sign versus biopsy, with sensitivity 68% and specificity 81%, reinforcing uncertainty rather than clear replacement (PMID: 30408588). Some listed support items are only indirectly relevant or weakly informative for the specific ultrasound-versus-biopsy replacement question, such as a case report using both modalities (PMID: 18494221) and a PET-focused paper rather than an ultrasound-biopsy comparison (PMID: 13130153). One recent registry abstract suggested sonography and MRI often confirm suspected GCA and biopsy is needed only exceptionally (PMID: 39389553), which conflicts with its placement in the support set and indicates heterogeneity in the evidence base.
contradict_summary: Several newer observational studies and narrative pieces argue that ultrasound can serve as a first-line test and may obviate biopsy in selected patients, particularly when performed by experienced operators. A retrospective cohort reported high performance for color duplex ultrasound against final clinical diagnosis (sensitivity 81%, specificity 98%) and concluded that biopsy may be unnecessary, especially when clinical suspicion is high or low (PMID: 27070887); another single-center series suggested a positive ultrasound in an appropriate clinical context can probably avoid biopsy (PMID: 35064750). The strongest contradictory abstract is the prospective multicenter EUREKA study, which found ultrasound sensitivity 94% and specificity 84% against 6-month clinical diagnosis and stated that vascular ultrasound might effectively replace biopsy as a first-line diagnostic method when done by systematically trained ultrasonographers with appropriate equipment (PMID: 38287632). However, several contradiction items are weaker for this exact replacement claim because they are pilot studies or case reports/reviews rather than robust comparative evidence, including a 10-patient pilot with 100% sensitivity/specificity (PMID: 21179222), vertebral artery sonography in stroke patients with confirmatory biopsy in all 5 cases (PMID: 21940968), and narrative/case-based articles advocating ultrasound based partly on guidelines rather than direct head-to-head evidence (PMID: 39335750; PMID: 39931599; PMID: 28352206; PMID: 31327543). Overall, the contradictory evidence shows promising ultrasound performance in some settings but is heterogeneous and often context-dependent.
conclusion: The statement is overall somewhat supported, because multiple comparative studies, reviews, and a meta-analysis describe uncertain or conflicting ultrasound-versus-biopsy performance and continue to treat biopsy as the reference standard. Although some newer studies—especially PMID: 38287632—suggest ultrasound could replace biopsy as a first-line test in expert settings, the total evidence remains heterogeneous and not definitive enough to conclude replacement broadly.

claim: In people with clinically suspected giant cell arteritis, temporal artery biopsy appears more sensitive than temporal artery ultrasound at comparable high specificity, although the evidence is very uncertain.
support_summary: Several studies and reviews report relatively low sensitivity for temporal artery ultrasound in suspected giant cell arteritis, often around 40% in routine practice, with moderate-to-high specificity, implying biopsy may detect more cases when specificity is kept high (PMID: 12186513, PMID: 23981759). One prospective comparison directly found biopsy sensitivity 73.7% and specificity 100% versus ultrasound sensitivity 42.6% and specificity 65.7%, favoring biopsy (PMID: 30318270). A meta-analysis of ultrasound versus biopsy reported pooled ultrasound sensitivity 68% and specificity 81%, and a review notes that several meta-analyses suggest TAB is more sensitive than ultrasound, while emphasizing conflicting literature and false-positive ultrasound results (PMID: 30408588, PMID: 32193868). Additional indirect support comes from a risk-stratification study in which low-risk patients were misclassified more often by ultrasound than biopsy, though this was not a direct sensitivity/specificity comparison (PMID: 36628005). Overall, the supporting evidence is mixed in strength, with the clearest support coming from older/smaller studies and narrative review statements rather than consistent high-quality head-to-head data.
contradict_summary: Multiple studies report high diagnostic performance of temporal artery or cranial/vascular ultrasound, with sensitivities ranging from about 81% to 100% and specificities from about 84% to 98%, in some cases suggesting ultrasound could replace biopsy as a first-line test (PMID: 15498914, PMID: 21179222, PMID: 27070887, PMID: 38287632). The strongest recent contradiction is the prospective multicentre EUREKA study, which found ultrasound sensitivity 94% versus temporal artery biopsy sensitivity 74%, although biopsy had higher specificity (100% vs 84%), so specificity was not truly comparable as stated in the claim (PMID: 38287632). Some contradictory items are weaker because they are case reports, narrative/guideline-style articles, or report confirmation rates rather than formal head-to-head sensitivity comparisons, limiting their evidentiary value and introducing categorization noise (PMID: 28352206, PMID: 39335750, PMID: 39389553, PMID: 39931599, PMID: 31729185). One Australian single-centre study reported ultrasound specificity around 93% and sensitivity 71.4% versus biopsy, again not directly showing biopsy to be more sensitive (PMID: 35064750). Major themes are that modern, expert-performed ultrasound can be highly sensitive and is increasingly used as a first-line test, but several contradictory abstracts do not strictly test the claim's 'comparable high specificity' condition.
conclusion: The claim is somewhat contradicted overall. Although some studies and reviews suggest temporal artery biopsy may be more sensitive than ultrasound, especially in older or routine-practice settings, more recent and stronger prospective evidence indicates ultrasound can be at least as sensitive or more sensitive than biopsy, even if biopsy often retains higher specificity; thus the evidence remains uncertain but leans against the claim.

claim: In adults undergoing surgery with general anaesthesia, erector spinae plane block probably provides only a slight, not clinically relevant reduction in postoperative pain at rest compared with no block.
support_summary: Several directly relevant randomized studies and one meta-analysis found little or no reduction in postoperative pain scores with erector spinae plane block (ESPB) versus no block or sham, despite occasional reductions in opioid consumption. In lumbar spine surgery, a double-blind RCT found no significant differences in 24-hour morphine use, pain scores over 5 days, or longer-term outcomes with ESPB versus saline sham (PMID: 35863786). A meta-analysis in adult cardiac surgery via sternotomy also found no significant reduction in postoperative pain at 4 or 12 hours (PMID: 36217996), and a nephrectomy trial reported no pain-score benefit over 24 hours despite lower tramadol use (PMID: 40926465). One listed “supporting” trial is a discrepancy because it compared ESPB with peri-articular injection rather than no block and found no superiority of ESPB, not a direct no-block comparison (PMID: 34861745).
contradict_summary: Most contradicting evidence consists of randomized trials and multiple meta-analyses reporting that ESPB reduces postoperative pain scores and/or opioid consumption compared with no block, especially in breast, thoracic, abdominal, and spine surgery. Examples include sham-controlled or no-block RCTs showing lower early resting pain after epigastric hernia repair (PMID: 30801359), lumbar spinal fusion (PMID: 33096517), bariatric surgery (PMID: 33295128), video-assisted thoracoscopic lobectomy (PMID: 37161305), and hysterectomy (PMID: 38095381), as well as several spine-surgery meta-analyses finding reductions in pain at multiple postoperative time points (PMID: 34593667, PMID: 35296129, PMID: 36800574). Some contradictions are weaker or not directly on-claim because they are case reports/series without controls or compare ESPB with another active block rather than no block (e.g., PMID: 29708919, PMID: 30522818, PMID: 35799555, PMID: 38677191, PMID: 41164615). Importantly, even among the stronger contradictory studies, several effects are modest and often concentrated in the early postoperative period, which creates tension with the claim’s wording about only slight, not clinically relevant benefit. Overall, however, the bulk of abstract-level evidence reports measurable analgesic benefit rather than no meaningful effect.
conclusion: The statement is overall more contradicted than supported. Although a few studies and at least one meta-analysis report little or no pain-score benefit, the larger body of randomized and pooled evidence across several surgical settings more often shows ESPB reduces early postoperative pain at rest and/or opioid use compared with no block, even if the clinical magnitude may vary by procedure and time point.

claim: In adults undergoing surgery with general anaesthesia, erector spinae plane block probably does not reduce postoperative pain at rest compared with placebo block.
support_summary: Three studies align with the claim that erector spinae plane block (ESPB) may not meaningfully reduce postoperative pain at rest, though direct placebo/sham comparisons are limited. In arthroscopic shoulder surgery, an RCT found no difference in resting pain in recovery and higher opioid use with ESPB versus peri-articular injection, but this is not a placebo comparison (PMID: 34861745). A meta-analysis in adult cardiac surgery via sternotomy found no significant reduction in postoperative pain at 4 or 12 hours with ESPB, although heterogeneity and small study numbers limit certainty (PMID: 36217996). In laparoscopic living-donor nephrectomy, a randomized study found no difference in pain scores over 24 hours despite lower tramadol consumption with ESPB versus no block, supporting the pain-specific part of the claim only partially (PMID: 40926465).
contradict_summary: Most contradictory evidence reports that ESPB reduces postoperative pain and/or opioid use versus no block or sham, especially in thoracic, breast, abdominal, gynecologic, and spine surgery. Several randomized trials directly showed lower early postoperative pain scores, including sham-controlled studies in open epigastric hernia repair and lumbar spinal fusion, with reduced pain at rest for up to about 12 hours but often diminishing by 24 hours (PMID: 30801359, PMID: 33096517). Multiple meta-analyses also found lower postoperative pain scores and opioid consumption versus no/sham block in thoracic, breast, cholecystectomy, and spine surgery populations, although effect sizes were sometimes modest and evidence quality low-to-moderate or heterogeneous (PMID: 32357842, PMID: 32609389, PMID: 34301447, PMID: 34593667, PMID: 35296129, PMID: 36609244, PMID: 36800574). Some items in this group are weaker or indirect because they are retrospective studies, case series/reviews, or compare ESPB with active comparators rather than placebo/sham, which should be interpreted cautiously despite being categorized as contradiction (e.g., PMID: 33828209, PMID: 37124664, PMID: 38054404, PMID: 39184663, PMID: 40047853).
conclusion: Overall, the claim is more contradicted than supported. Although a few studies and one cardiac-surgery meta-analysis found no significant pain-score benefit, the larger body of evidence—particularly sham/no-block randomized trials and multiple meta-analyses across several surgeries—suggests ESPB often reduces early postoperative pain at rest and opioid consumption, even if benefits may be short-term and procedure-specific.

claim: In adults undergoing surgery with general anaesthesia, erector spinae plane block appears to have no important advantage over other regional anaesthetic techniques for postoperative pain at rest, including paravertebral block and transversus abdominis plane block.
support_summary: One directly relevant randomized non-inferiority trial in lung surgery found continuous erector spinae plane block (ESPB) was not non-inferior to paravertebral block (PVB), with higher mean pain scores over 24 hours, more analgesic failures, and greater opioid use in the ESPB group, supporting the view that ESPB offers no important advantage over PVB and may be worse in this setting (PMID: 37646584). Several items in the contradicting set are actually directionally consistent with the claim, notably a nephrectomy non-inferiority trial showing similar pain-at-rest outcomes between ESPB and thoracic PVB despite some procedural advantages for ESPB (PMID: 36692339), and abdominal-surgery/laparoscopic cholecystectomy meta-analyses reporting statistically better pain or opioid outcomes versus TAP but differences below clinical-significance thresholds or similar outcomes versus other blocks (PMID: 35848431; PMID: 39135713; PMID: 36609244). Overall, the abstract-level evidence that directly supports the claim is limited but includes one strong head-to-head PVB trial plus some miscategorized comparative studies suggesting little clinically important advantage.
contradict_summary: Most contradictory items do not directly test the claim because many compare ESPB with no block or sham and simply show ESPB is effective versus control, which does not establish an advantage over other regional techniques (e.g., PMID: 36938107; PMID: 37277703; PMID: 32317873; PMID: 34935999). Among genuinely comparative studies versus TAP, several randomized trials and meta-analyses report lower pain scores, reduced opioid consumption, or longer analgesia with ESPB in abdominal surgery, bariatric surgery, liver resection, laparoscopic cholecystectomy, and urologic surgery (PMID: 32449336; PMID: 36154912; PMID: 37987727; PMID: 38256522; PMID: 40240902; PMID: 40491767; PMID: 41164615; PMID: 41417185). However, there are important discrepancies: some of these reported differences were statistically but not clinically significant, and at least one thoracic meta-analysis concluded ESPB had no clinically important differences versus other techniques despite some safety advantages, which actually aligns partly with the claim rather than contradicting it (PMID: 34301447; PMID: 35848431; PMID: 39135713). Comparative evidence against the claim is therefore substantial for ESPB versus TAP in some abdominal procedures, but much weaker or absent for a broad statement across all surgeries and for PVB specifically.
conclusion: The statement is overall somewhat supported, but the evidence is mixed. Direct evidence versus paravertebral block and several clinically interpreted meta-analyses suggest ESPB has no important analgesic advantage and may even be inferior in some settings, whereas multiple abdominal-surgery comparisons versus TAP report statistically better outcomes for ESPB, though these are not always clinically important and many other cited studies are only versus no block.
