contradict_summary: Contradicting evidence is limited but includes one systematic review/meta-analysis focused on contemporary HFrEF trials that found no significant effect of exercise-based cardiac rehabilitation on all-cause hospitalization or HF hospitalization at 6 or 12 months (PMID: 31177833). The other item appears miscategorized as contradiction: it actually states that exercise-based cardiac rehabilitation improves long-term hospitalization outcomes and does not address a lack of short-term hospitalization benefit, only short-term mortality (PMID: 38391275). Thus, the main contradictory theme is that in more recent HFrEF-focused analyses, hospitalization benefits were not statistically significant, possibly reflecting narrower populations and fewer studies for these endpoints.
conclusion: Overall, the claim is more supported than contradicted. The best available evidence includes multiple randomized trials and especially the Cochrane review showing reduced short-term all-cause and HF-related hospital admissions, whereas the main contrary evidence is a narrower HFrEF-specific meta-analysis and one misclassified review that does not truly contradict the hospitalization claim.

claim: In adults with heart failure, exercise-based cardiac rehabilitation compared with no exercise control likely improves health-related quality of life in the short term.
support_summary: Multiple randomized trials and meta-analyses in adults with heart failure report short-term improvements in health-related quality of life with exercise-based cardiac rehabilitation versus usual care/no exercise, including the large HF-ACTION trial showing a modest but significant early KCCQ benefit at 3 months that persisted thereafter (PMID: 19351942), a 19-trial Cochrane review/meta-analysis showing improved HRQoL versus usual care (PMID: 20393935; PMID: 20494922), and a home-based CR meta-analysis concluding short-term HRQoL improvement versus usual care (PMID: 27441476). Additional controlled studies also found better quality-of-life outcomes after about 8–12 weeks or 3 months, including multidisciplinary CR and resistance/circuit-based programs (PMID: 20498608, PMID: 39570919, PMID: 40971218). Many other supporting items are reviews or uncontrolled before-after studies that are directionally consistent but less directly aligned with the claim’s comparator or outcome design (for example PMID: 24653758, PMID: 30166886, PMID: 33673950). One supporting meta-analysis noted significant benefit on KCCQ but not consistently on MLHFQ, suggesting some heterogeneity by instrument and study design rather than uniform effects across all QoL measures (PMID: 31177833).
contradict_summary: A small randomized trial in CHF found improved exercise capacity, anxiety, and depression after 8 weeks of aerobic training but no change in its quality-of-life questionnaire, directly contradicting the claim for short-term QoL benefit (PMID: 18084172). A 2023 systematic review/meta-analysis of 11 RCTs reported no additional quality-of-life benefit of comprehensive outpatient CR beyond comparison groups, despite gains in walk distance and oxygen consumption (PMID: 37658777). However, one listed contradicting review actually states that cardiac rehabilitation enhances quality of life and mainly notes no short-term mortality benefit, so its placement in the contradicting set appears discrepant (PMID: 38391275). Another contradicting item concerns heart transplant recipients rather than adults with heart failure, limiting its relevance to the claim despite reporting no short-term HRQoL effect (PMID: 28375548).
conclusion: Overall, the statement is more supported than contradicted. The strongest evidence includes a very large RCT and multiple heart-failure-specific systematic reviews/meta-analyses showing short-term HRQoL improvement with exercise-based cardiac rehabilitation, whereas contradictory evidence is smaller, more heterogeneous, or less directly applicable.

claim: In adults with advanced non-small cell lung cancer receiving first-line chemotherapy, adding the TG4010 vaccine may slightly improve progression-free survival but probably makes little or no difference in overall survival or serious treatment-related adverse events compared with chemotherapy alone.
support_summary: Two clinical trial abstracts directly support a modest progression-free survival benefit from adding TG4010 to first-line chemotherapy in advanced NSCLC. In the phase 2B trial, 6-month PFS was 43.2% with TG4010 plus chemotherapy versus 35.1% with chemotherapy alone, while serious adverse events occurred in 52.1% versus 47.2%, suggesting no large difference in serious toxicity despite more low-grade fever, abdominal pain, and injection-site pain with TG4010 (PMID: 22019520). In the randomized double-blind TIME phase 2b trial, median PFS was 5.9 versus 5.1 months (HR 0.74), and no serious adverse event was deemed related to TG4010 alone or to the TG4010-treatment combination; grade 3-4 treatment-related events were not increased versus placebo (PMID: 26727163). A review article also summarizes that TG4010 showed better PFS when added to cisplatin-gemcitabine, but it does not add primary data on overall survival or serious adverse events (PMID: 25030654). Across these abstracts, the main recurring theme is slight PFS improvement with little evidence of a meaningful increase in serious treatment-related toxicity, while direct abstract-level evidence on overall survival is limited.
contradict_summary: No contradicting evidence items were provided.
conclusion: The statement is overall more supported than contradicted. The available trial abstracts consistently suggest a modest PFS benefit and no clear increase in serious treatment-related adverse events with TG4010, but abstract-level evidence for little or no overall survival benefit is limited rather than definitive.

claim: In adults with advanced non-small cell lung cancer receiving maintenance treatment after chemotherapy, the racotumomab vaccine may improve overall survival compared with placebo, while making little or no difference in progression-free survival or serious treatment-related adverse events.
support_summary: A randomized placebo-controlled trial found that racotumomab-alum used as switch maintenance after first-line chemotherapy improved overall survival versus placebo in advanced NSCLC (median 8.23 vs 6.80 months; HR 0.63), with reported adverse events mainly local/systemic symptoms and no clear signal in the abstract of excess serious toxicity (PMID: 24788102). However, that same trial also reported significantly longer progression-free survival with racotumomab (5.33 vs 3.90 months; HR 0.73), which conflicts with the claim's statement that PFS changes little or not at all, so this supportive item is internally mixed/discrepant (PMID: 24788102). A Bayesian network meta-analysis of maintenance strategies also suggested racotumomab-alum may be among the best regimens for overall survival, though its PFS estimate also trended favorable and the comparison was indirect rather than placebo-specific (PMID: 27781159). A real-world observational study reported promising overall survival for racotumomab maintenance versus historical controls, but it did not directly address placebo comparison, PFS, or serious treatment-related adverse events, so it only weakly supports the claim's OS component (PMID: 41150423).
contradict_summary: The main contradicting evidence is a review summarizing Phase II/III randomized double-blind trial data and stating that racotumomab-alum significantly improved both overall survival and progression-free survival versus placebo, directly opposing the claim that PFS may show little or no difference (PMID: 25420897). This contradiction is reinforced by the underlying randomized trial abstract itself, although it was placed in the support set, because it explicitly reports a statistically significant PFS benefit with racotumomab versus placebo (PMID: 24788102). No abstract provided contradicts the claim's suggestion that serious treatment-related adverse events are not materially increased.
conclusion: The statement is overall only partially supported: evidence is fairly consistent that racotumomab may improve overall survival compared with placebo, but the available randomized-trial evidence does not support the idea of little or no PFS difference and instead suggests a significant PFS benefit. Evidence on serious treatment-related adverse events is limited in these abstracts but does not show a clear major safety contradiction.

claim: In adults with advanced non-small cell lung cancer receiving chemotherapy, adding the SRL172 vaccine may not improve overall survival and may increase serious treatment-related adverse events compared with chemotherapy alone.
support_summary: No supporting evidence items were provided.
contradict_summary: One small randomized phase II study reported a trend toward better response and survival with SRL172 plus chemotherapy, with similar chemotherapy toxicity and only mild injection-site inflammation, but this was based on just 28 evaluable patients with mixed NSCLC/mesothelioma and was not definitive (PMID: 10970684). The key phase III randomized trial found no statistically significant overall survival benefit from adding SRL172, although quality of life improved; this item is therefore discrepant with its placement in the contradicting set because its abstract actually supports the 'may not improve overall survival' part of the claim and does not show increased serious adverse events (PMID: 15151947). A secondary analysis/review likewise states the initial phase III analysis showed no overall survival improvement, while suggesting possible benefit in adherent patients with adenocarcinoma; it does not address serious treatment-related adverse events and is therefore only a limited contradiction at most (PMID: 17928219). Another contradicting item is clearly off-topic: it studies serplulimab, not SRL172, and should not be used as evidence about the SRL172 vaccine (PMID: 38181795). Overall, the contradicting set contains one small suggestive trial, one off-target study, and two abstracts that actually align at least partly with lack of overall survival benefit.
conclusion: The statement is overall more supported than contradicted, although direct evidence for increased serious treatment-related adverse events is weak from the provided abstracts. The strongest relevant evidence is the phase III SRL172 trial showing no overall survival improvement (PMID: 15151947), whereas the main contrary evidence is a small phase II trend signal (PMID: 10970684) and a secondary subgroup analysis (PMID: 17928219).

claim: In people undergoing elective stoma reversal, purse-string skin closure likely reduces surgical site infection compared with linear skin closure.
support_summary: The supporting evidence is extensive and consistent: multiple retrospective/prospective comparative studies and randomized trials report lower SSI rates with purse-string closure than with linear/primary closure after stoma reversal, often with large absolute differences (for example 0% vs 21.4% in PMID: 25745624, 2.9% vs 21.8% in PMID: 27626025, 3/58 vs 11/59 in PMID: 38676785, and 17 vs 3 infections in PMID: 29657907). Several meta-analyses and systematic reviews also found strongly reduced SSI with purse-string closure, with pooled effect estimates around OR/RR 0.08-0.18 and consistent findings across RCTs and observational studies (PMID: 23354904, PMID: 30074070, PMID: 30547183, PMID: 30809775, PMID: 39213201, PMID: 40188299, PMID: 41786646, PMID: 41486907). Recurring secondary themes are improved scar cosmesis/patient satisfaction but sometimes slower wound healing with purse-string closure (PMID: 25745624, PMID: 27626025, PMID: 36405661, PMID: 41786646). Some support items are weaker or indirect, such as a narrative review noting only weak evidence (PMID: 29670882), a gunsight-vs-linear study not directly about purse-string closure (PMID: 34567568), and a technical note outside standard elective stoma reversal comparisons (PMID: 35875285). One apparent discrepancy exists within the support set: PMID: 37978002 states purse-string had significantly lower SSI, but the abstract reports percentages in the opposite direction, suggesting likely reporting inconsistency.
contradict_summary: The contradicting evidence is smaller and mixed. Two studies found no statistically significant SSI advantage for purse-string closure despite numerically lower SSI with purse-string: 5.6% vs 16.7% in PMID: 21602963 and 2.7% vs 6.9% in PMID: 37567817, with both concluding outcomes did not significantly differ by closure technique. One randomized trial comparing purse-string approximation with primary closure plus drain also showed no significant SSI difference (5.0% vs 8.9%; PMID: 30367238), though this comparison is not strictly linear closure and mainly favored the drain group on wound healing rather than SSI. The strongest contradictory signal is PMID: 38186536, which reported numerically higher SSI with purse-string (30% vs 15%), but this difference was also not statistically significant. Overall, the contradiction theme is less that linear closure is better and more that some individual studies were underpowered or failed to show a significant SSI benefit for purse-string closure.
conclusion: The claim is overall more supported. The evidence base includes numerous comparative studies and several meta-analyses showing consistently lower SSI rates with purse-string closure versus linear/primary closure after elective stoma reversal, whereas the contradictory studies are fewer and mostly report nonsignificant differences rather than convincing evidence of harm or superiority of linear closure.

claim: In people undergoing elective stoma reversal, purse-string skin closure may increase patient satisfaction compared with linear skin closure.
support_summary: Multiple randomized/prospective studies and meta-analyses report higher patient-reported satisfaction or better scar/cosmesis with purse-string closure than linear closure after elective stoma reversal. A systematic review/meta-analysis found greater cosmetic satisfaction with purse-string approximation based on three studies (PMID: 23354904), and a later comprehensive meta-analysis of 14 studies likewise concluded patient satisfaction was higher with purse-string closure (PMID: 30074070). Several individual trials/prospective studies directly reported significantly better satisfaction or scar-related approval with purse-string closure, including PMIDs 25745624, 27626025, 29657907, 32476717, 33819218, and 36405661; some newer RCTs support this indirectly through better scar quality/cosmesis rather than explicit satisfaction measures (PMIDs: 37978002, 38676785). A few supportive items are only indirectly relevant or less generalizable, such as a technical note not about elective stoma reversal (PMID: 35875285) and pediatric studies (PMIDs: 36542007, 37978002), but the overall pattern in the abstracts favors improved patient-perceived cosmetic outcomes with purse-string closure.
contradict_summary: Contradictory evidence exists from a randomized trial showing no significant difference in patient satisfaction between purse-string and linear closure after ileostomy reversal despite lower SSI with purse-string closure (PMID: 29149428). A systematic review of eight RCTs reported satisfaction was lower with purse-string closure in the first postoperative week but no different at 1 and 6 months, concluding overall quality of life was similar between groups (PMID: 30547183). This creates a discrepancy with the supportive meta-analyses and trials, suggesting any satisfaction advantage may depend on timing of assessment or which patient-reported outcome is measured.
conclusion: The statement is overall more supported than contradicted. Most abstracts, including multiple randomized/prospective studies and two meta-analyses, report better patient satisfaction or scar cosmesis with purse-string closure, although at least one RCT and one systematic review suggest the advantage may be absent or limited over longer follow-up.

claim: In people undergoing elective stoma reversal, purse-string skin closure may have little or no effect on incisional hernia or operative time compared with linear skin closure.
support_summary: Multiple systematic reviews/meta-analyses of randomized or mixed study designs consistently report no statistically significant difference between purse-string and linear/conventional skin closure for operative time and incisional hernia after stoma reversal, while showing lower surgical-site infection with purse-string closure (PMID: 30547183, PMID: 29474885, PMID: 30074070, PMID: 39213201, PMID: 41486907). The most directly relevant review found incisional hernia RR 0.53 (95% CI 0.08-3.53) and operative time MD -0.06 with no significant difference (PMID: 30547183). A smaller comparative study also found similar operative times, although it did not report incisional hernia outcomes, so it only partially supports the claim (PMID: 21602963). A narrative review noted only weak evidence linking purse-string closure to lower incisional hernia risk indirectly via reduced SSI and emphasized the limited evidence base for hernia outcomes, which is broadly compatible with little/no clear effect rather than strong benefit (PMID: 29670882). Overall, the supporting literature is dominated by pooled analyses showing no clear difference in the two claim-specific outcomes.
contradict_summary: One retrospective single-center study using follow-up CT after ileostomy reversal reported a significantly lower incisional hernia rate with purse-string closure versus direct stitch/standard closure (12.9% vs 35.2%, p=0.017), directly contradicting the claim for hernia outcomes (PMID: 29523989). However, this study did not address operative time in the abstract and is observational, so its contradiction is limited to incisional hernia and is lower-level than the meta-analytic evidence. There is also a terminology discrepancy because the comparator is described as direct stitch/standard closure rather than explicitly linear closure, though it is likely similar.
conclusion: The statement is overall more supported. Several meta-analyses consistently show no significant difference in incisional hernia or operative time between purse-string and linear/conventional closure, whereas the contradictory evidence consists mainly of a single retrospective study suggesting reduced hernia risk with purse-string closure.

claim: In women undergoing surgical repair of vaginal prolapse, permanent transvaginal mesh, compared with native tissue repair, reduces awareness of prolapse and recurrent prolapse on examination and lowers repeat surgery for prolapse.
support_summary: The strongest support comes from a large Cochrane review of 37 RCTs, which found that permanent transvaginal mesh versus native tissue repair reduced awareness of prolapse, recurrent prolapse on examination, and repeat surgery for prolapse, although it also increased mesh-related harms and repeat surgery for the composite of prolapse/SUI/mesh exposure (PMID: 26858090). A retrospective study in women with recurrent prolapse reported lower vaginal bulge symptoms, lower anatomic recurrence, and lower prolapse reoperation rates with transvaginal mesh than repeat native tissue repair, but total reoperation was similar once mesh-complication surgery was included (PMID: 27324758). An older small comparative study of severe recurrent anterior prolapse also found fewer recurrences with synthetic mesh than without mesh (PMID: 8987927). Other supportive items are weaker because they are reviews or uncontrolled series, and one appears misclassified because it concerns Surgisis/biologic material rather than permanent transvaginal synthetic mesh and lacks a direct native-tissue comparison (PMID: 22291710).
contradict_summary: Several RCTs and long-term follow-up studies found no meaningful symptomatic advantage of transvaginal mesh over native tissue repair, despite some better anatomic results, and often no reduction in repeat surgery; examples include no significant reduction in recurrence at 12 months (PMID: 19583714), no difference in 3-year cure rates with all recurrent prolapse reoperations occurring in the mesh group in one trial (PMID: 24084533), similar 7-year subjective recurrence despite lower anatomic recurrence and no reduction in reintervention (PMID: 31506808), no 6-year benefit in the PROSPECT trial with additional mesh-complication surgery (PMID: 36018353), and no subjective benefit but more further operations at 12 years in recurrent prolapse (PMID: 36662272). Large observational data also challenge the claim: a claims-based study found similar 5-year surgery for recurrent prolapse but higher overall repeat surgery with mesh because of mesh revision/removal (PMID: 23400940), and a nationwide follow-up found similar long-term reoperation risk despite lower bulge symptoms after transvaginal mesh (PMID: 41277806). Some reviews explicitly conclude that evidence does not support transvaginal mesh for apical or posterior prolapse, or that benefits are limited by morbidity (PMID: 27696355, PMID: 37493538, PMID: 29502352), while one item in the contradiction set is actually discordant with its label because it states native tissue repair had increased awareness, repeat surgery for prolapse, and recurrence versus polypropylene mesh for anterior compartment prolapse, even though overall authors still cautioned against mesh because of morbidity (PMID: 27901278). A recurring theme is that mesh may improve anatomic recurrence in selected compartments but this does not consistently translate into lower symptomatic recurrence or lower overall repeat surgery once mesh complications are counted.
conclusion: The statement is only partially supported and overall the evidence is mixed, with stronger contradiction regarding the claim that permanent transvaginal mesh lowers repeat surgery for prolapse-related care overall. While there is credible evidence—especially older RCT meta-analysis data—that mesh can reduce awareness of prolapse and anatomic recurrence, multiple longer-term studies show little or no symptomatic advantage and no clear reduction in reoperation once mesh-exposure/removal procedures are considered.

claim: In women undergoing surgical repair of vaginal prolapse, permanent transvaginal mesh, compared with native tissue repair, increases total repeat surgery, bladder injury, and de novo stress urinary incontinence.
support_summary: The strongest supporting evidence is a large Cochrane review of 37 RCTs comparing transvaginal mesh/grafts with native tissue repair, which found that permanent transvaginal mesh increased the combined outcome of repeat surgery for prolapse, stress incontinence, or mesh exposure, as well as bladder injury and de novo stress urinary incontinence versus native tissue repair (PMID: 26858090). Earlier surgical reviews/meta-analyses similarly reported higher reoperation after transvaginal polypropylene mesh for multicompartment prolapse and increased de novo stress urinary incontinence with transobturator mesh, although some abstracts emphasize repeat surgery driven partly by mesh exposure rather than prolapse recurrence alone (PMID: 23633316). Individual comparative studies also support increased de novo stress urinary incontinence after mesh versus colporrhaphy (PMID: 19731311), increased further operations over 12 years after vaginal mesh versus native tissue repair in recurrent prolapse (PMID: 36662272), and higher rates of de novo urinary incontinence and reoperation with vaginal mesh-augmented cystocele repair in a national database study (PMID: 31078822). Population-based Scottish data found anterior/posterior mesh prolapse repair associated with more later complications and more further incontinence/prolapse surgery than non-mesh repair, reinforcing a higher repeat-surgery burden (PMID: 28010993). Several other supportive items are narrative reviews or indirect evidence and are weaker than the randomized/systematic-review data.
contradict_summary: Contradictory evidence is limited and mixed. One randomized trial in posthysterectomy vaginal vault prolapse found lower prolapse recurrence with mesh than sacrospinous fixation and no difference in de novo stress urinary incontinence, but it did not directly report total repeat surgery or bladder injury, so its relevance to the full claim is partial (PMID: 23021692). A small uncontrolled case series of 20 mesh repairs reported no bladder injuries or de novo stress urinary incontinence, but because it lacked a native-tissue comparator it is a major categorization discrepancy and provides weak contradiction at best (PMID: 24843196). A retrospective single-center comparison found no increase in overall perioperative/postoperative adverse events with transvaginal mesh versus native tissue repair, though it did not specifically establish lower repeat surgery, bladder injury, or de novo stress urinary incontinence with mesh (PMID: 29633262). A recent nationwide follow-up study reported similar long-term prolapse reoperation rates after native tissue and mesh-augmented surgery, with numerically lower rates after transvaginal mesh, but it addressed prolapse reoperation rather than the broader 'total repeat surgery' outcome and did not address bladder injury or de novo stress urinary incontinence (PMID: 41277806).
conclusion: Overall, the claim is more supported than contradicted. The most persuasive evidence comes from randomized-trial syntheses and long-term comparative studies showing increased combined repeat surgery burden, bladder injury, and de novo stress urinary incontinence with permanent transvaginal mesh versus native tissue repair, whereas the contradictory studies are fewer, often narrower in outcome scope, or methodologically less directly relevant.

claim: In women undergoing surgical repair of vaginal prolapse, absorbable transvaginal mesh and biological grafts do not show clear benefit over native tissue repair for prolapse-related outcomes or quality of life.
support_summary: Multiple reviews and randomized evidence indicate that biologic grafts and absorbable/partially absorbable transvaginal mesh generally do not provide clear patient-important advantages over native tissue repair. A Cochrane review of anterior compartment prolapse found few or no differences between biologic graft and native tissue repair, and concluded biologic grafts or absorbable mesh offer minimal advantage (PMID: 27901278); another Cochrane review found no difference for biologic grafts in prolapse awareness, repeat surgery, dyspareunia, or quality of life, and only limited/very low-quality evidence that absorbable mesh might reduce examination recurrence without enough evidence for other outcomes or QoL (PMID: 26858090). A randomized trial of partially absorbable mesh versus native tissue repair showed no significant anatomical, composite, or global improvement benefit at 24 months (PMID: 30159720), and the large PROSPECT randomized trial found no clinically important difference in prolapse symptoms or functional cure at 4-6 years between native tissue, biologic xenograft, and mesh-augmented repairs (PMID: 36018353). Posterior compartment reviews similarly report that augmented repair, including biologic grafts or absorbable mesh, has not shown superiority to native tissue repair (PMID: 30466706; PMID: 29502352), and a long-term cohort found biologic graft augmentation did not confer significant benefit over posterior colporrhaphy and had higher subjective failure (PMID: 32910081). Several supportive items are narrative reviews or discuss permanent mesh rather than the claim's specific absorbable mesh/biologic graft comparison, so they are indirect rather than definitive evidence.
contradict_summary: Contradicting evidence is limited and mixed. A recent small single-arm pilot study of partially absorbable mesh reported excellent 6-month anatomical results and improved quality-of-life scores, but it did not compare outcomes against native tissue repair, so it does not directly refute the claim despite being categorized as contradiction (PMID: 40954994). One review stated that absorbable mesh augmentation improved anatomical outcomes and biologic grafts improved anatomical outcomes without subjective benefit in anterior compartment repair (PMID: 24142055), but this mainly supports possible anatomic advantages rather than clear overall prolapse-related or quality-of-life benefit. Another systematic review actually stated biologic grafts or synthetic absorbable mesh did not provide an advantage in any compartment, conflicting with its placement in the contradiction group (PMID: 27275813). The remaining contradictory items largely concern transvaginal mesh in general or recurrent prolapse, not specifically absorbable mesh or biologic grafts, and they report some better anatomic/symptom recurrence outcomes offset by mesh-related reoperations or no overall reoperation advantage (PMID: 27324758; PMID: 28759459).
conclusion: The statement is overall more supported. The strongest evidence comes from systematic reviews and randomized trials showing no clear benefit of biologic grafts and no convincing patient-centered or quality-of-life advantage for absorbable/partially absorbable transvaginal mesh over native tissue repair, whereas the contradictory evidence is either indirect, limited to anatomic outcomes, non-comparative, or mismatched to the claim.

claim: In people with leptospirosis, antibiotics compared with placebo or no intervention have very uncertain effects on all-cause mortality and on non-serious adverse events, and no included trial reported serious adverse events for this comparison.
support_summary: One Cochrane review on antibiotic prophylaxis for leptospirosis reported that, versus placebo, doxycycline may result in little to no difference in all-cause mortality based on low-certainty evidence, while non-serious adverse events may be increased but the evidence is very uncertain; importantly, none of the included trials reported serious adverse events (PMID: 38483067). However, this review concerns prophylaxis in people at risk of leptospirosis rather than treatment in people with established leptospirosis, so it only indirectly supports the claim and represents a population/intervention mismatch relative to the statement from PMID: 38483092.
contradict_summary: No contradicting evidence items were provided.
conclusion: The statement is only weakly supported by the provided evidence. The sole supporting item matches the direction of uncertainty about mortality, non-serious adverse events, and absence of reported serious adverse events, but it addresses antibiotic prophylaxis rather than treatment in people with leptospirosis, limiting its direct applicability.

claim: In people with leptospirosis, different antibiotics compared with each other have very uncertain effects on all-cause mortality, serious adverse events, and non-serious adverse events.
support_summary: One recent Cochrane review of antibiotic prophylaxis for leptospirosis found only a single head-to-head trial of doxycycline versus azithromycin, and for that comparison mortality was not reported, serious adverse events were not reported, and non-serious adverse events showed no clear difference with very low-certainty evidence (PMID: 38483067). The abstract explicitly states that for one antibiotic versus another, effects on relevant outcomes were very uncertain and that none of the included trials reported serious adverse events (PMID: 38483067). However, this is a partial indirect match to the claim because the review is about prophylaxis rather than treatment of people with established leptospirosis, so applicability to the claim population is uncertain.
contradict_summary: No contradicting evidence items were provided.
conclusion: The statement is weakly supported overall because the only cited evidence reports very low-certainty, sparse head-to-head antibiotic data and missing mortality/serious adverse event reporting, which is consistent with uncertainty. However, the evidence is indirect since it concerns prophylaxis rather than confirmed leptospirosis cases, limiting confidence in the claim as stated.

claim: Randomized trial evidence is insufficient to support a clear benefit of antibiotic treatment for leptospirosis infection on clinically important outcomes.
support_summary: Multiple systematic reviews/meta-analyses of randomized trials consistently conclude that trial evidence is insufficient to recommend for or against antibiotics for leptospirosis because available studies are few, small, and often at high risk of bias (PMID: 10796767; PMID: 20091518; PMID: 22336839; PMID: 23930159; PMID: 34578157). Across these reviews, antibiotics generally did not show statistically significant benefit for mortality, hospital stay, dialysis/oliguria, or other major clinical outcomes, although some analyses suggested possible reductions in prolonged hospitalization, leptospiruria, or duration of illness/fever with wide uncertainty (PMID: 10796767; PMID: 22336839; PMID: 23930159). Several non-systematic or indirect sources also state that treatment guidance remains unclear and more trials are needed, but these are weaker evidence for the specific claim (PMID: 10742727; PMID: 23982231; PMID: 27075788; PMID: 39100284). A few listed support items are actually about prophylaxis rather than treatment, so their relevance to this treatment claim is limited and should be interpreted as a discrepancy in categorization (PMID: 38483067; PMID: 28587195; PMID: 39058190). Overall, the dominant theme is uncertainty and lack of clear benefit on clinically important outcomes from randomized treatment evidence.
contradict_summary: The strongest contradictory item is not a direct refutation of the claim: one network meta-analysis reported that penicillin ranked highest for efficacy, but the abstract does not clearly demonstrate improved clinically important outcomes such as mortality or hospital stay, limiting how strongly it contradicts the statement (PMID: 28056834). Two randomized head-to-head trials found ceftriaxone, cefotaxime, doxycycline, and penicillin had similar outcomes, which mainly informs comparative choice among antibiotics rather than proving antibiotics are beneficial versus no antibiotic treatment (PMID: 12802748; PMID: 15546074). A recent review/network meta-analysis reported shorter defervescence time with several antibiotics but no reduction in mortality or hospital stay, so this is only a partial contradiction because the claim is about clinically important outcomes (PMID: 38627798). One review article advocates early diagnosis and early antibiotic therapy, but as a narrative review it provides less direct evidence from randomized trials in the abstract (PMID: 41520794). Another supposedly contradictory study actually concerns prophylaxis, not treatment, and therefore is misaligned with the treatment claim despite suggesting preventive benefit in a high-risk military setting (PMID: 19588324).
conclusion: The statement is overall more supported. The best available evidence here consists of multiple systematic reviews of randomized trials that repeatedly find no clear, reliable benefit of antibiotics on major clinical outcomes and emphasize low-quality, heterogeneous, and limited trial data, whereas the contradictory items are either indirect, focused on comparative regimens, or about prophylaxis rather than treatment.

claim: In older adults with depression residing in long-term care facilities, psychological therapies compared with usual care or other non-therapy control conditions may reduce depressive symptoms, but the evidence is very uncertain.
support_summary: Several facility-based intervention studies reported lower depressive symptoms with psychological or psychosocial approaches versus usual care or wait-list controls, including reminiscence therapy (PMID: 15578667), story-centred care with effects sustained to 3 months (PMID: 29554111), and acceptance and commitment therapy (PMID: 26942691). Additional quasi-experimental studies of nonpharmacologic interventions in long-term care also found between-group improvements in depression, such as a musical fitness programme (PMID: 34227165) and binaural beat music with photic stimulation versus sham (PMID: 38558209), though these are not all clearly psychological therapies in the strict sense. Broader reviews suggest psychological treatment in older adults can help, but also explicitly note limited trials and very low-quality evidence for some modalities such as CBT and reminiscence/life review in frail elderly populations (PMID: 27537217), which aligns with the claim’s uncertainty. Some items in the support set are only background reviews or study protocols without outcome data (PMID: 18261699, PMID: 31676658, PMID: 32164587, PMID: 37700236), and one light-therapy study found no significant between-group difference despite within-group improvement (PMID: 24750268), so this set contains weaker or misclassified evidence.
contradict_summary: Direct contradictory evidence includes a pilot nursing-home study in dementia care units finding no difference in depressive symptoms between behavioral treatment and usual care despite improvement in behavioral disturbance (PMID: 15933281), and a recent randomized trial of tactile massage in long-term care that improved comfort/relaxation but not depressive symptoms (PMID: 39312625). However, several articles placed in the contradicting set actually support benefit rather than contradiction: the PMAL cluster RCT reported significant depression reduction in long-term care residents (PMID: 30295729), and a network meta-analysis in long-term care found multiple nonpharmacologic treatments, including CBT and reminiscence-type approaches, improved depressive symptoms versus usual care (PMID: 39730115). Two additional meta-analyses in broader elderly or inpatient populations also reported moderate-to-large benefits of psychological treatments (PMID: 16955421; PMID: 21599897), but these are less directly applicable to long-term care residents. Overall, the contradiction set is weakened by substantial misclassification and by the fact that only two studies directly show null effects in relevant settings.
conclusion: The statement is overall more supported than contradicted. Multiple directly relevant long-term care studies suggest psychological or related nonpharmacologic interventions can reduce depressive symptoms, while at least one systematic review also emphasizes that the certainty of this evidence is very low, although some newer evidence appears stronger and the dataset contains several misclassified studies on both sides.

claim: In older adults with depression residing in long-term care facilities, psychological therapies compared with usual care or other non-therapy control conditions may improve quality of life and psychological well-being in the short term, but the evidence is very uncertain.
support_summary: Several studies in long-term care settings suggest short-term psychological or nonpharmacologic interventions can improve depressive symptoms, mood, or related well-being measures versus control conditions. In institutionalized older adults, individual reminiscence therapy improved depressive symptoms and mood over 4 months versus control (PMID: 15578667), and a pilot wait-list-controlled trial found acceptance and commitment therapy reduced depression scores at 8 weeks with effects maintained at 3 months (PMID: 26942691). A recent quasi-experimental pilot reported improved vitality, mental health, and depressive mood after 2 weeks of binaural beat music plus rhythmic photic stimulation versus sham, though this intervention may not cleanly fit "psychological therapy" and daytime care centers may not fully match long-term residential care (PMID: 38558209). Indirect evidence includes a meta-analysis in older adults overall showing moderate to large antidepressant effects of psychological treatments, but it was not specific to long-term care facilities or quality-of-life outcomes (PMID: 16955421), and a review of depression in long-term care states psychological therapies may be valuable without providing direct comparative data (PMID: 18261699). One item categorized as supportive is discrepant: light therapy showed within-group improvement but no significant between-group difference versus routine care, so it offers at best weak support and could be interpreted as contradictory for comparative benefit (PMID: 24750268).
contradict_summary: No explicit contradicting evidence items were provided. However, one study in the support set found no significant between-group difference in depression or sleep disruption for light therapy versus routine care despite within-group improvement, which weakens the overall supportive signal and represents a classification discrepancy rather than clear support (PMID: 24750268). There is also little direct abstract-level evidence here specifically for quality of life outcomes in depressed long-term care residents, as most studies focus on depressive symptoms or mood rather than quality of life.
conclusion: The statement is overall more supported than contradicted, because multiple studies in long-term care settings report short-term improvements in depressive symptoms, mood, vitality, or mental health with psychological or related nonpharmacologic interventions versus control conditions. Still, the evidence remains limited and somewhat uncertain due to small or quasi-experimental studies, indirect evidence, variable intervention types, and sparse direct data on quality of life.

claim: In older adults with depression residing in long-term care facilities, psychological therapies compared with non-therapy control conditions may increase treatment dropout, suggesting possible lower acceptability, but the evidence is very uncertain.
support_summary: No supporting evidence items were provided.
contradict_summary: A pilot randomized trial in long-term care residents found ACT was well accepted, with high treatment satisfaction reported by residents and staff, and the abstract does not indicate excess dropout in the therapy arm, which conflicts with the claim of lower acceptability (PMID: 26942691). A very large routine-care study reported that older adults had lower attrition from psychological therapy than working-age adults, but this is only indirectly relevant because it was not limited to long-term care residents or specifically to depression in that setting (PMID: 34274792). A qualitative study of behavioural activation in older adults with long-term conditions found high acceptability and manageable burden, but it was conducted in community/NHS contexts during COVID-19 rather than among depressed long-term care residents, so it is also indirect evidence and a setting mismatch (PMID: 36914198). Overall, the contradicting evidence mainly points to acceptable psychological interventions and no clear sign of increased dropout, though two studies are only partially applicable to the specific claim.
conclusion: The statement is overall more contradicted than supported based on the evidence provided. However, the contradiction is only moderate because only one study directly involves long-term care residents, while the other studies address broader older-adult populations or different settings.

claim: In adults undergoing cardiac surgery with cardiopulmonary bypass, prophylactic corticosteroids probably do not reduce all-cause mortality compared with placebo or no treatment.
support_summary: Multiple reviews and meta-analyses consistently report no mortality benefit from prophylactic corticosteroids in adults undergoing cardiac surgery with cardiopulmonary bypass. A Cochrane-style meta-analysis of 54 randomized trials found no reduction in mortality with corticosteroids versus placebo/no treatment (OR 1.12, 95% CI 0.65-1.92) and concluded there was no beneficial effect on mortality or major cardiac/pulmonary complications (PMID: 21563145). A larger 2019 meta-analysis of 62 RCTs including 16,457 patients likewise found no significant mortality difference (OR 0.96, 95% CI 0.81-1.14), explicitly concluding that prophylactic corticosteroids did not reduce mortality (PMID: 31972000). Narrative reviews also state that clinical benefits are unproven or absent and may even include harms, though these are less direct than trial/meta-analytic evidence for mortality (PMID: 11888978, PMID: 24715740). One large RCT of dexamethasone did not reduce a composite endpoint including death rather than mortality alone, so it is supportive but indirect for the specific mortality claim (PMID: 23117776); similarly, a post-hoc analysis focused on rethoracotomy is only indirectly relevant despite noting no major benefit in the parent trial (PMID: 26319483).
contradict_summary: No contradicting evidence items were provided. No abstract in the supplied set presents direct evidence that prophylactic corticosteroids reduce all-cause mortality in this population.
conclusion: The statement is overall more supported. This is based on consistent evidence from multiple systematic reviews/meta-analyses and the absence of any supplied contradictory studies, with the strongest direct evidence showing no significant mortality reduction from prophylactic corticosteroids in adult cardiac surgery with cardiopulmonary bypass.

claim: In adults undergoing cardiac surgery with cardiopulmonary bypass, prophylactic corticosteroids may increase myocardial complications but may reduce pulmonary complications compared with placebo or no treatment.
support_summary: One large systematic review/meta-analysis of 62 randomized trials in adults undergoing cardiac surgery with cardiopulmonary bypass found that prophylactic corticosteroids were associated with a higher incidence of myocardial adverse events (OR 1.17, 95% CI 1.03-1.33) and a lower incidence of pulmonary adverse events (OR 0.86, 95% CI 0.75-0.98) versus placebo, directly matching the claim (PMID: 31972000). The same abstract notes uncertainty in the clinical meaning of the myocardial finding because myocardial endpoints varied across trials, but the direction of effect supports the statement.
contradict_summary: Several evidence items weaken or partially contradict the claim, mainly on the myocardial-risk component. A broader 2022 meta-analysis reported that in adults, low-dose corticosteroids did not increase myocardial infarction risk and concluded such dosing provided benefits without increasing myocardial infarction, although the abstract does not clearly address pulmonary complications and also includes pediatric studies, making it only a partial contradiction to the full claim (PMID: 35722531). An earlier meta-analysis of 54 randomized studies found no significant effect on either myocardial complications (OR 0.95, 95% CI 0.57-1.60) or pulmonary complications (OR 0.83, 95% CI 0.49-1.40), contradicting both the proposed myocardial increase and pulmonary reduction (PMID: 21563145). A large randomized trial of intraoperative dexamethasone found no reduction in the composite of major adverse events and did not show clear evidence in the abstract of increased myocardial complications, though shorter ventilation duration could be seen as indirect pulmonary benefit rather than contradiction; thus this item is mixed and somewhat miscategorized as purely contradicting (PMID: 23117776).
conclusion: The claim is supported, but only modestly, because the most directly relevant and largest adult CPB meta-analysis found both increased myocardial adverse events and reduced pulmonary adverse events with prophylactic corticosteroids (PMID: 31972000). However, the evidence is not definitive since earlier and dose-specific analyses reported no myocardial increase and no clear pulmonary benefit, and one large RCT did not clearly corroborate the myocardial-harm signal.

claim: In adults undergoing cardiac surgery with cardiopulmonary bypass, prophylactic corticosteroids may reduce infectious complications compared with placebo or no treatment, while effects on gastrointestinal bleeding, renal failure, and hospital length of stay are little to no difference or very uncertain.
support_summary: Three higher-level evidence sources suggest prophylactic corticosteroids may reduce infectious complications after adult cardiac surgery with cardiopulmonary bypass. A large RCT found dexamethasone was associated with reduced postoperative infection and shorter ICU/hospital stay, while showing no significant reduction in major adverse events including renal failure (PMID: 23117776). An individual-patient meta-analysis of 11,989 participants reported significantly lower infection risk with steroids, no significant difference in renal failure, and shorter ICU/hospital stay (PMID: 31401207). A systematic review/meta-analysis of 62 RCTs also found lower surgical site infection with corticosteroids and no mortality benefit; however, the abstract does not clearly report gastrointestinal bleeding, renal failure, or hospital-stay estimates, so support for those parts of the claim is incomplete from this item (PMID: 31972000).
contradict_summary: Contradicting evidence is mixed and partly discrepant with its labeling. An older meta-analysis reported reduced ICU and hospital stay and did not highlight excess infectious or gastrointestinal complications in the abstract, so it does not clearly contradict the claim and may be miscategorized (PMID: 18664462). Another meta-analysis found no benefit on major outcomes and reported an increase in gastrointestinal bleeding with corticosteroids, which conflicts with the claim's suggestion of little to no difference or uncertainty for that outcome; however, the abstract does not address infection directly (PMID: 21563145). A more recent meta-analysis reported no significant reduction in infection, but did find reduced renal failure and shorter ICU stay, which contradicts the infection component yet also conflicts with the claim's suggestion of little to no difference/uncertainty for renal outcomes (PMID: 35657725).
conclusion: Overall, the statement is somewhat more supported than contradicted. The strongest and largest contemporary evidence consistently suggests corticosteroids reduce postoperative infection, while evidence on gastrointestinal bleeding and renal failure is conflicting or uncertain, and length-of-stay findings are not fully aligned with the claim because some studies report shorter stays rather than little to no difference.

claim: In infants younger than 24 months with bronchiolitis, high-flow nasal cannula therapy compared with low-flow oxygen therapy may shorten hospital stay and duration of oxygen therapy.
support_summary: A small observational comparative study in infants under 12 months with moderate-severe bronchiolitis found faster clinical improvement with HFNC, with about 2 fewer oxygen days and 3 fewer hospital days versus low-flow oxygen (PMID: 27102726). Broader supportive material includes reviews/guidelines stating HFNC is more effective than low-flow oxygen for bronchiolitis or citing RCTs favoring HFNC, but these abstracts usually do not specifically report shorter length of stay or oxygen duration and often focus instead on reduced treatment failure or overall effectiveness (PMID: 30488718, PMID: 33790048, PMID: 30940682). Some observational before-after studies also reported shorter hospital stay after HFNC introduction, but they did not directly compare HFNC with low-flow oxygen and therefore only indirectly support the claim (PMID: 20036376, PMID: 28544665). One systematic review/network meta-analysis is mixed: it reports HFNC reduced oxygen days and treatment failure versus low-flow oxygen in some analyses, yet also concludes experimental evidence does not support HFNC as superior initial treatment and notes heterogeneity for oxygen days and hospital stay, making this item internally discrepant despite being categorized as support (PMID: 36661419). Overall, direct abstract-level support for shorter hospital stay and oxygen duration comes mainly from limited observational data, with many supportive items being indirect or non-specific.
contradict_summary: Several higher-level evidence sources directly contradict the claim: a meta-analysis of 9 RCTs found no significant difference in hospital length of stay or duration of oxygen supplementation with HFNC versus other oxygen therapies (PMID: 30655267), and a systematic review of prospective RCTs concluded HFNC does not seem to shorten oxygen need or hospital admission duration (PMID: 32232547). A large multicenter randomized trial in infants under 12 months found HFNC reduced escalation of care, but showed no significant difference in duration of hospital stay or oxygen therapy versus standard oxygen therapy (PMID: 29562151). Earlier review evidence similarly found no clear difference in total duration of oxygen therapy, time to discharge, or total length of stay, although certainty was low because only one small pilot trial was available (PMID: 24442856). Additional observational and implementation studies reported no improvement or even longer hospital stay with HFNC use, though these are more vulnerable to confounding by severity and some do not directly compare HFNC with low-flow oxygen (PMID: 36066264, PMID: 38775344, PMID: 37818204). The contradiction is therefore driven mainly by randomized and systematic review evidence showing no consistent reduction in length of stay or oxygen duration.
conclusion: The statement is overall more contradicted than supported. Although a small observational comparative study and some indirect reviews suggest possible benefit, the stronger evidence base—especially systematic reviews and a large RCT—does not show consistent shortening of hospital stay or duration of oxygen therapy with HFNC versus low-flow oxygen in infants with bronchiolitis.

claim: In infants younger than 24 months with bronchiolitis, high-flow nasal cannula therapy compared with low-flow oxygen therapy probably reduces the need for escalation of respiratory treatment.
support_summary: The strongest direct support comes from a large multicenter RCT showing lower escalation of care with high-flow versus standard oxygen in infants with bronchiolitis treated outside the ICU (12% vs 23%; PMID: 29562151). A systematic review/meta-analysis including children under 24 months also found lower treatment failure with high-flow versus low-flow oxygen (RR 0.46, moderate-certainty evidence; PMID: 34086669). Additional smaller comparative studies and RCTs reported faster clinical improvement and/or trends toward less intubation with high-flow compared with standard/low-flow oxygen (PMID: 27102726, PMID: 35493278). Several retrospective studies and reviews report reduced intubation or low ICU admission after HFNC implementation, but many are indirect because they do not directly compare with low-flow oxygen and are more vulnerable to confounding (PMID: 20036376, PMID: 21369809, PMID: 28544665). Overall, the support is driven mainly by one large RCT plus a meta-analysis, while many other supportive items are observational, narrative, or indirect.
contradict_summary: Contradicting evidence includes a randomized trial in infants under 6 months with moderate bronchiolitis that found no statistically significant reduction in treatment failure/escalation with HFNC versus control (14% vs 20%; PMID: 32381496). A recent systematic review/network meta-analysis reported that although HFNC reduced treatment failure versus low-flow oxygen in some analyses, the authors concluded that experimental evidence does not show clear advantages of high-flow as initial treatment over low-flow oxygen, creating an internal discrepancy within this item (PMID: 36661419). Observational studies found HFNC use associated with longer hospital stay and/or more ICU admissions, but these are likely confounded by clinicians preferentially using HFNC in sicker infants and therefore do not cleanly test the claim (PMID: 36066264, PMID: 38775344). Multiple reviews emphasize uncertainty, inconsistent benefits across outcomes, and lack of clear superiority over low-flow oxygen in recent trials, though these reviews often also acknowledge possible reduction in treatment failure in selected patients (PMID: 38964936, PMID: 24442856, PMID: 33498527, PMID: 41674761). Thus, the contradictory evidence is mixed, with one directly negative RCT and several cautionary or confounded review/observational reports.
conclusion: The statement is overall more supported than contradicted. This judgment is based mainly on the large direct RCT and supportive meta-analysis showing reduced treatment failure/escalation with HFNC versus low-flow/standard oxygen, whereas the contradicting literature includes one negative RCT but otherwise relies heavily on reviews or observational data with confounding and mixed interpretations.

claim: In infants younger than 24 months with bronchiolitis, high-flow nasal cannula therapy compared with low-flow oxygen therapy does not appear to change the risk of adverse events.
support_summary: Direct comparative evidence generally suggests no clear difference in adverse events between HFNC and standard/low-flow oxygen. A Cochrane review found one small RCT with no adverse events in either group, though the evidence was low quality and underpowered (PMID: 24442856). A large multicenter RCT reported one pneumothorax in each arm (<1%), indicating similar serious adverse-event rates despite lower escalation of care with HFNC (PMID: 29562151). A systematic review/meta-analysis of 9 RCTs concluded HFNC appeared safe and did not show significant differences in major outcomes such as ICU transfer or intubation versus other oxygen therapies, although the abstract is truncated and not specifically centered on adverse events (PMID: 30655267). Several additional supportive items are observational studies or narrative/systematic reviews emphasizing feasibility and absence of reported harms rather than direct HFNC-vs-low-flow adverse-event comparisons, so they provide only indirect support and create some relevance/miscategorization concerns (PMID: 23900520, PMID: 31102551, PMID: 36661419, PMID: 38964936).
contradict_summary: Most contradicting items do not directly show higher adverse-event risk with HFNC; instead, they mainly report better efficacy or less treatment failure/PICU admission with HFNC, which is not the same as adverse events. A pilot comparison found no adverse events with HFNC and fewer PICU admissions than standard low-flow oxygen, so this actually conflicts with its placement as contradiction for the specific adverse-event claim (PMID: 24612137). Other studies and reviews reported faster clinical improvement, reduced treatment failure, lower PICU admission, or possible lower intubation with HFNC versus low-flow oxygen, but they did not demonstrate a difference in adverse events per se (PMID: 27102726, PMID: 34086669, PMID: 33342682, PMID: 35493278). The guideline states HFNC is safe and more effective than low-flow oxygen for moderate-severe bronchiolitis, again addressing effectiveness rather than increased or decreased adverse-event risk (PMID: 33790048). Overall, this set is weakened by a substantial mismatch between the claim's focus on adverse events and the cited studies' focus on treatment failure or clinical benefit.
conclusion: The statement is overall more supported. The best direct evidence includes a large RCT and a Cochrane review showing no clear difference in serious adverse events between HFNC and standard oxygen, whereas most supposedly contradicting studies address efficacy or treatment failure rather than adverse-event risk.

claim: In people with established cardiovascular disease receiving mobile phone text message interventions to improve medication adherence, compared with usual care, the effects on medication adherence are very uncertain.
support_summary: A cardiovascular-focused systematic review found that, although text messaging may help secondary prevention, the evidence base was small and heterogeneous and the authors concluded that reliable conclusions about effects could not be drawn (PMID: 29803297). A pilot RCT in acute coronary syndrome patients found no statistically significant difference in medication adherence between SMS plus usual care and usual care alone at 60 days, suggesting possible lack of effect but with limited precision due to small sample size (PMID: 33988519). Overall, the supporting evidence emphasizes imprecision, heterogeneity, and limited trial size rather than clear benefit or harm.
contradict_summary: Several cardiovascular studies and reviews report beneficial effects of mobile or SMS-based interventions on adherence: an RCT in coronary heart disease found significantly better medication adherence with a text-message/web cardiac rehabilitation program (PMID: 26490012), and a post-MI pilot trial found a 14.2 percentage-point improvement with daily text reminders versus usual care (PMID: 28778843). Cardiovascular-focused reviews generally conclude mHealth can improve medication adherence, although benefits are sometimes modest and trial quality is limited (PMID: 29474713; PMID: 36975590). However, some items in this contradicting set are only partially relevant to the claim because they concern chronic disease broadly rather than established cardiovascular disease (PMID: 26831740; PMID: 27437126), hypertension or dyslipidemia populations that may not match established CVD (PMID: 32432556; PMID: 35342691; PMID: 38366854), non-SMS modalities such as apps or inbuilt phone reminders (PMID: 35800910; PMID: 40336435), or mixed eHealth interventions where SMS alone showed no significant difference versus usual care (PMID: 39008845), creating an important discrepancy in this evidence set. An umbrella review nevertheless reported highly suggestive effects of text messages on medication adherence in people with cardiovascular disease, though not all outcomes across chronic diseases were significant (PMID: 39332937).
conclusion: The statement is somewhat more contradicted than supported. While there is real uncertainty from small, heterogeneous cardiovascular studies and at least one null pilot trial, multiple cardiovascular RCTs and reviews suggest text-message-based interventions can improve adherence, though the strength of contradiction is tempered by several partially off-target or mixed-modality studies.

claim: In people with established cardiovascular disease receiving mobile phone text message interventions to improve medication adherence, compared with usual care, text messaging may have little or no effect on fatal cardiovascular events, non-fatal cardiovascular events, or combined cardiovascular events.
support_summary: No non-zero supporting evidence items were available.
contradict_summary: No non-zero contradicting evidence items were available.
conclusion: This statement had only zero-score or missing-score rows in the input file.

claim: In people with established cardiovascular disease receiving mobile phone text message interventions to improve medication adherence, compared with usual care, text messaging may have little or no effect on low-density lipoprotein cholesterol, blood pressure, or heart rate.
support_summary: Direct evidence in patients with established cardiovascular disease generally supports little or no effect of medication-adherence text messaging on these physiologic outcomes. The strongest study is the TXT2HEART Colombia RCT, which found no significant effect on LDL-C, systolic/diastolic blood pressure, or heart rate after 12 months in patients with ASCVD (PMID: 34319247). Similarly, the CHAT trial in coronary heart disease found no significant differences in systolic blood pressure or LDL-C at 6 months (PMID: 30998400). Additional studies showing no blood pressure benefit with text messaging exist, but several are only partially relevant because they enrolled hypertension or mixed chronic disease populations rather than people with established cardiovascular disease (PMID: 26769742, PMID: 31396815, PMID: 37327024).
contradict_summary: Contradictory evidence includes one highly relevant coronary heart disease RCT, TEXT ME, which reported significantly lower LDL-C and systolic blood pressure with lifestyle-focused text messaging versus usual care, directly opposing the claim for those outcomes (PMID: 26393848). Several other studies and reviews also report blood pressure improvements with text messaging, but most are less directly applicable because they focus on hypertension populations, primary prevention, or broad chronic disease/mobile-phone interventions rather than established cardiovascular disease receiving medication-adherence texting (PMID: 33078730, PMID: 34550078, PMID: 35342691, PMID: 39332937, PMID: 40186180). One cited contradiction is only a trial protocol and provides no outcome data, so it should be treated as weak/inappropriate contradiction despite its placement in this group (PMID: 31818831). Overall, contradiction mainly concerns blood pressure and some LDL-C benefit in certain interventions, with little direct abstract-level evidence on heart rate.
conclusion: The statement is overall more supported than contradicted. This is because the most directly relevant medication-adherence trials in established cardiovascular disease largely found no meaningful effect on LDL-C, blood pressure, or heart rate, whereas much of the contradictory evidence comes from less comparable populations or different types of text-message interventions, though one notable CHD trial did show benefit.

claim: In adults with coronary heart disease or heart failure, psychological interventions compared with no psychological intervention probably reduce depression and anxiety.
support_summary: Most supporting evidence comes from multiple systematic reviews/meta-analyses in coronary heart disease showing small-to-moderate reductions in depression and anxiety versus usual care/no psychological intervention, including Cochrane reviews with significant pooled effects for depression and anxiety (PMID: 21833943, PMID: 23179678, PMID: 29212370) and an updated review across CHD/HF/AF that also found reduced depression and anxiety, though with high heterogeneity (PMID: 39172083). Heart-failure-specific support is narrower but includes a meta-analysis of CBT showing improved depression versus usual care in HF, without clear anxiety data (PMID: 28733911). Several smaller trials in CHD reported improved depression/anxiety with psychotherapy, counseling, psycho-cardiology, mind-body exercise, or nursing/education-based psychosocial programs compared with routine care or untreated controls (e.g., PMID: 19049687, PMID: 32796784, PMID: 32831874, PMID: 36776943, PMID: 39949339). A recurring theme is benefit for psychological symptoms despite little or no effect on mortality or major cardiac events (PMID: 21833943, PMID: 29212370, PMID: 34328010, PMID: 39172083). Some items in the support list are only indirect reviews, protocols, or non-psychological/ambiguous interventions, so they provide weaker support than the major meta-analyses.
contradict_summary: Contradictory evidence includes a heart-failure dyadic psychoeducational RCT that found no effect on depressive symptoms or other patient outcomes at 24 months (PMID: 26406475) and a meta-analysis of nurse-led support/lifestyle programs in CHD showing no significant improvement in depression or anxiety versus usual care (PMID: 30170464). A CBT sub-analysis in CHD found improvement in somatic anxiety but not depression, suggesting mixed symptom effects rather than broad benefit (PMID: 28195501). Another meta-analysis found that adding psychological interventions to exercise-based cardiac rehabilitation did not clearly improve anxiety and showed only a trend toward less depression (PMID: 30857429). One mindfulness meta-analysis actually reports significant reduction in depression but not anxiety versus inactive controls (PMID: 33433036), so despite being placed in the contradict group it is only a partial contradiction and illustrates misclassification/discrepancy in the evidence grouping.
conclusion: Overall, the statement is more supported than contradicted. The strongest evidence comes from several systematic reviews/meta-analyses—especially in coronary heart disease—showing modest reductions in depression and anxiety, while contradictory studies tend to be intervention-specific, null for only some outcomes, or partially misclassified.

claim: In adults with coronary heart disease or heart failure, psychological interventions compared with no psychological intervention may improve mental health-related quality of life but have little to no effect on physical health-related quality of life.
support_summary: The strongest direct support comes from a recent Cochrane systematic review/meta-analysis of adults with CHD, HF, or AF, which found psychological interventions improved mental HRQoL at 6–12 months but not physical HRQoL, alongside reductions in depression and anxiety (PMID: 39172083). Several HF or CHD studies/reviews also report improvements in psychological outcomes, psychological/social QoL domains, or overall QoL after relaxation, mindfulness, supportive-educative nursing, or dual-heart/interactive nursing interventions, but many do not clearly separate mental from physical HRQoL or do not report physical HRQoL effects directly (PMID: 20423422; PMID: 31635481; PMID: 33433036; PMID: 34328010; PMID: 15573333; PMID: 32796784; PMID: 38206716). Some evidence is only indirect or mechanistic rather than intervention-based, such as associations of hostility with mental but not physical HRQoL in CHD (PMID: 26777887), and one narrative HF article is more descriptive than outcome-based (PMID: 12418434). Overall, the recurring theme is fairly consistent mental-health benefit, while evidence for physical HRQoL benefit is absent, inconsistent, or not demonstrated. There is also a discrepancy in this support set because several included studies support only the mental-health portion of the claim and do not directly verify the "little to no effect" on physical HRQoL.
contradict_summary: Some studies suggest broader programs incorporating psychological support may improve both mental and physical or overall QoL, especially when psychological components are embedded within secondary prevention, Tai Chi, home cardiac rehabilitation, or psychologically assisted cardiac rehabilitation (PMID: 23012159; PMID: 24404785; PMID: 38515387; PMID: 40495983). However, several of these are not clean tests of psychological intervention alone versus no psychological intervention, because the intervention includes exercise, rehabilitation, education, or lifestyle components that could drive physical benefits (PMID: 23012159; PMID: 24404785; PMID: 38515387; PMID: 40495983). Other contradiction-group items are actually indirect prognostic or observational evidence about links between psychological distress and physical function rather than trials showing psychological interventions improve physical HRQoL (PMID: 39552880; PMID: 18310649), and one review instead reports mixed/null effects, including no HRQoL improvement in one HF self-management program (PMID: 26181666), while one long-term dyadic HF trial found no effect on health or depressive symptoms (PMID: 26406475). Thus, the main contradictory theme is possible physical benefit in multimodal or rehab-based interventions, but much of this evidence is indirect or mismatched to the claim. There is substantial discrepancy in this set because several articles do not directly contradict the specific claim as stated.
conclusion: The statement is overall more supported than contradicted. The most directly relevant and highest-quality evidence is the Cochrane review showing improved mental HRQoL but not physical HRQoL (PMID: 39172083), whereas much of the contradictory evidence comes from multimodal rehabilitation programs or indirect observational studies that do not cleanly test psychological intervention alone against no psychological intervention.

claim: In adults with coronary heart disease or heart failure, psychological interventions compared with no psychological intervention probably have little to no effect on all-cause mortality and may have little to no effect on major adverse cardiovascular events.
support_summary: Several systematic reviews/meta-analyses in coronary heart disease consistently report no strong evidence that psychological interventions reduce total/all-cause mortality, while improving depression and anxiety symptoms (PMID: 15106183, PMID: 34328010). The most directly relevant and recent Cochrane review across CHD, heart failure, and atrial fibrillation found no benefit for all-cause mortality or major adverse cardiovascular events, despite improvements in depression, anxiety, and mental health-related quality of life (PMID: 39172083). An additional narrative review notes that trials treating depression in CHD have generally not shown reliable improvement in cardiac event-free survival, which indirectly supports limited cardiovascular benefit (PMID: 39048281). Major themes are symptomatic psychological benefit without clear mortality/MACE benefit, along with concerns about heterogeneity, trial quality, and publication bias that weaken any apparent signal for reduced non-fatal events (PMID: 15106183).
contradict_summary: Some evidence suggests possible cardiovascular benefit in subsets or specific outcomes rather than a clear null effect: a mixed-treatment comparison reported that interventions with behavioral components were associated with lower odds of all-cause mortality and nonfatal myocardial infarction in CHD (PMID: 19258485), and one randomized AMI study reported fewer adverse cardiovascular events with Psycho-Cardiology therapy (PMID: 36776943). However, two items placed in the contradicting group actually largely align with the claim on the main outcomes, because they found no strong evidence for reduced total mortality, myocardial infarction, or revascularization, though they did report a small reduction in cardiac/cardiovascular mortality (PMID: 23179678, PMID: 29212370). An older review also described decreased clinical cardiac events during the active treatment phase in two trials but emphasized insufficient long-term data (PMID: 7810188). Overall, the contradicting evidence is mixed and partly miscategorized, with the main recurring theme being possible benefit for cardiac-specific outcomes or selected intervention components, not robust evidence for all-cause mortality or broad MACE reduction.
conclusion: The statement is overall more supported than contradicted. The strongest and most directly relevant evidence, especially recent systematic reviews/meta-analyses, indicates little to no effect on all-cause mortality and no clear reduction in major adverse cardiovascular events, whereas contradictory evidence is limited, outcome-specific, or partly inconsistent with its own categorization.

claim: In people undergoing gastrointestinal surgery, preoperative parenteral nutrition probably has little to no clear effect on postoperative non-infectious complications, infectious complications, or length of hospital stay compared with usual care.
support_summary: Several randomized trials found no significant reduction in postoperative infectious complications, overall complications, or hospital stay with perioperative/preoperative immunonutrition versus standard care in gastrointestinal surgery, including elective GI surgery (PMID: 17461312), upper GI cancer surgery where parenteral and enteral strategies showed similar outcomes (PMID: 18650630), colon cancer surgery (PMID: 34353994), and a small pilot GI cancer surgery trial (PMID: 41754168). Across these studies, complication rates and length of stay were broadly comparable between groups. However, these studies mostly evaluated oral or perioperative immunonutrition rather than preoperative parenteral nutrition specifically, so they are indirect for the claim. This creates a scope discrepancy: the direction of effect is consistent with the claim, but the intervention often does not exactly match it.
contradict_summary: Directly relevant contradictory evidence includes older and review-level literature suggesting benefit from preoperative or perioperative parenteral nutrition, especially in malnourished or upper-GI patients: a meta-analysis of glutamine-supplemented PN found reduced infectious complications and shorter hospital stay after abdominal surgery (PMID: 23635587), an older study reported fewer septic complications with preoperative PN in malnourished GI surgical patients (PMID: 3132010), and a narrative review concluded most included studies showed reduced postoperative complications and sometimes shorter stay with preoperative PN in selected malnourished patients (PMID: 32384662). Additional contradictory items report benefits of perioperative nutrition or immunonutrition more broadly, including reduced complications and/or length of stay (PMID: 24676327, PMID: 31062270, PMID: 38474706, PMID: 38638718), but many of these are not specific to preoperative parenteral nutrition and instead concern oral/enteral immunonutrition, multimodal prehabilitation, or guideline/review statements. There is also internal discrepancy within the contradicting set: one review of intravenous nutrition states that most randomized trials failed to show benefit overall, with only low-quality evidence for preoperative PN benefit in upper GI cancer (PMID: 17524720), which actually aligns partly with the claim despite being categorized as contradiction. Overall, the contradictory evidence is stronger on selected subgroups and supplemented PN formulations than on routine preoperative PN versus usual care across all GI surgery patients.
conclusion: The evidence is mixed but leans slightly toward the claim for routine use across general gastrointestinal surgery populations, because several randomized studies show no clear benefit, while much of the contradictory evidence reflects selected malnourished patients, glutamine-supplemented PN, or broader immunonutrition rather than preoperative PN itself. Still, contradiction remains substantial due to meta-analytic and subgroup evidence suggesting benefit in specific high-risk populations.

claim: In people undergoing gastrointestinal surgery, preoperative enteral nutrition and immune-enhancing nutrition probably have little to no clear effect on postoperative complications or length of hospital stay compared with usual care.
support_summary: Three randomized trials in gastrointestinal surgery/cancer populations found no clear postoperative benefit from preoperative or perioperative nutrition strategies on complications or hospital stay. A factorial RCT in elective upper gastrointestinal surgery reported no significant differences in infectious complications, overall morbidity, mortality, or length of stay with immunomodulatory formulas versus standard diets, and no advantage of enteral over parenteral feeding (PMID: 18650630), though this study focused postoperative rather than strictly preoperative immunonutrition. A small RCT of 7-day preoperative immunonutrition before GI tumor surgery improved prealbumin but found no significant effect on postoperative complications or hospital stay (PMID: 19152452).
