Another systematic review reported reduced odds of fluorosis when fluoride toothpaste use began after 12, 14, or 24 months and stated that ingesting pea-sized amounts or more can lead to mild fluorosis (PMID: 24487610). However, some of these contradictory items also contain internal inconsistency or only partial contradiction, because frequency often was not associated with fluorosis and some age effects varied across studies and eras (PMID: 9758424, PMID: 18474053).
conclusion: The statement is overall somewhat contradicted. Although one higher-level systematic review emphasizes that the evidence is weak, biased, and equivocal for some exposures, multiple studies and reviews identify earlier starting age—and in some settings larger toothpaste amounts—as associated with increased fluorosis risk, while evidence on brushing frequency remains less convincing.

claim: In adults receiving peripherally inserted central catheters, integrated valve technology compared with peripherally inserted central catheters with no valve appears to make little or no difference to venous thromboembolism, occlusion, catheter failure, or all-cause mortality.
support_summary: Two randomized studies found no clear advantage of valved versus non-valved PICCs for key complications: a 180-patient trial in adults receiving chemotherapy reported occlusions and thromboses were evenly distributed and concluded there were no clinical advantages of valved PICCs (PMID: 25198813), and the ELeCTRiC randomized study found valve presence/type did not significantly affect occlusion rates (PMID: 22505280). A retrospective study in lung cancer patients undergoing femoral central venous catheterization also found no significant differences in irreversible obstruction, catheter-related thrombosis, accidental dislodgment, or indwelling time, though some obstruction subtypes and breakage differed between catheter types (PMID: 29701120). Across these studies, the recurring theme is little or no difference in thrombosis and several failure-related outcomes, with strongest direct evidence for occlusion and some evidence for thrombosis/catheter removal. None of the supporting abstracts directly address all-cause mortality.
contradict_summary: One older randomized trial reported fewer overall occlusive or infectious complications with valved versus clamped non-valved PICCs, with numerically fewer occlusions and bloodstream infections in the valved group, suggesting benefit from valve technology (PMID: 10541127). Another randomized trial does not directly compare valved versus non-valved catheters; instead it compares proximal-valved versus distal-valved PICCs and found markedly fewer fractures with proximal valves and a nonsignificant trend toward fewer occlusive/infectious complications (PMID: 11585883), so its placement in the contradicting set is only partially relevant to the claim. Overall, the contradicting evidence mainly concerns occlusion/infection or durability rather than venous thromboembolism or mortality, and part of it reflects comparator/design differences rather than a clean valve-versus-no-valve contrast.
conclusion: The statement is overall more supported than contradicted. The best-matched newer randomized evidence and one retrospective comparison generally show little or no difference between valved and non-valved PICCs for occlusion and some thrombosis/failure outcomes, whereas the contradicting evidence is older and partly less directly aligned with the claim or focused on different valve designs rather than valve versus no valve.

claim: In adults receiving peripherally inserted central catheters, anti-thrombogenic surface-modified catheters compared with non-modified peripherally inserted central catheters may make little or no difference to venous thromboembolism, occlusion, catheter failure, catheter breakage, or all-cause mortality, and evidence is very uncertain for catheter-associated bloodstream infection.
support_summary: No non-zero supporting evidence items were available.
contradict_summary: No non-zero contradicting evidence items were available.
conclusion: This statement had only zero-score or missing-score rows in the input file.

claim: In adults receiving peripherally inserted central catheters, antimicrobial-impregnated catheters compared with non-antimicrobial catheters probably make little or no difference to occlusion, catheter-related bloodstream infection, or all-cause mortality, and may make little or no difference to venous thromboembolism, catheter failure, or catheter breakage.
support_summary: Direct PICC evidence is limited but includes one randomized trial finding no significant difference between chlorhexidine-impregnated and non-impregnated PICCs for CLABSI or venous thromboembolism, although moderate bleeding was higher with the antimicrobial PICC (PMID: 27240799). Two older non-PICC central venous catheter studies/reviews also reported no significant reduction in bloodstream infection or patient outcomes with antimicrobial impregnation (PMID: 12830410; PMID: 9364263). Overall, the supporting evidence mainly aligns with the claim for little or no difference in bloodstream infection, mortality, and possibly VTE, but most of it comes from broader CVC populations rather than adults specifically receiving PICCs. None of the supporting abstracts directly address occlusion, catheter failure, or catheter breakage. This creates an applicability gap between the claim and much of the cited evidence.
contradict_summary: Most contradicting evidence comes from non-PICC central venous catheter trials, meta-analyses, and reviews reporting reduced catheter-related bloodstream infection or colonization with antimicrobial-impregnated catheters, especially for chlorhexidine/silver sulfadiazine or minocycline/rifampin devices (PMID: 9265424; PMID: 9918482; PMID: 11008998; PMID: 28475779; PMID: 23740696). One retrospective PICC study reported markedly lower CLABSI risk with antimicrobial-impregnated PICCs versus non-antimicrobial PICCs, though this was observational rather than randomized (PMID: 30180989). Several items are indirect or less suitable for the exact claim because they are reviews, cost-effectiveness models, or comparisons between two antimicrobial catheters rather than antimicrobial versus non-antimicrobial PICCs (PMID: 12548250; PMID: 9878638; PMID: 27152256; PMID: 33742573), so there is a relevance discrepancy within this group. Even among these contradicting studies, mortality benefit is generally not shown, and adverse outcomes such as thrombosis may not differ (PMID: 23740696; PMID: 28475779). There is little direct abstract-level evidence here on occlusion, catheter failure, or catheter breakage.
conclusion: The statement is overall somewhat contradicted, mainly because a larger body of evidence reports reduced catheter-related bloodstream infection with antimicrobial catheters. However, much of that evidence is from general central venous catheters rather than PICCs, while the most directly relevant randomized PICC study found no difference, so certainty for the exact PICC-specific claim remains mixed.

claim: In adults undergoing major open vascular, endovascular, or hybrid vascular surgery, preoperative coronary revascularization added to usual care appears to have little or no effect on preventing perioperative acute myocardial infarction compared with usual care alone, although the evidence is very uncertain.
support_summary: The strongest direct evidence is a randomized trial analysis showing that among 462 vascular surgery patients, preoperative coronary revascularization did not reduce 30-day death or nonfatal myocardial infarction overall or in higher-risk subgroups, including those with ischemia on stress testing (PMID: 20031818). Two review articles are broadly consistent with this interpretation: one concludes preoperative coronary revascularization should generally be limited to patients with unstable cardiac symptoms rather than used routinely before vascular surgery (PMID: 16917424), and another describes the benefit versus medical therapy as controversial based on existing studies (PMID: 18292413). Overall, the supportive evidence centers on lack of demonstrated perioperative cardiac benefit from routine prophylactic revascularization, but part of this support comes from narrative reviews rather than new comparative data.
contradict_summary: One study often framed as contradictory compared CABG versus PCI only among patients already selected for preoperative coronary revascularization and found fewer perioperative myocardial infarctions after subsequent vascular surgery with CABG than PCI; however, this does not directly test revascularization versus usual care alone, so its contradiction to the claim is limited and reflects a categorization discrepancy (PMID: 16928485). A recent observational study reported that identifying silent ischemia and performing ischemia-targeted coronary revascularization plus best medical therapy was associated with fewer adverse cardiovascular events and improved 5-year survival versus best medical therapy alone, but the abstract emphasizes long-term outcomes and does not clearly establish a perioperative acute myocardial infarction benefit specifically (PMID: 38518962). Another small retrospective study found fewer combined adverse outcomes in men with positive stress tests who underwent coronary intervention or received beta-blockers compared with no intervention, but it assessed a composite outcome, was sex-specific, and did not isolate perioperative acute myocardial infarction (PMID: 21195358). Thus, the contradicting set suggests possible benefit in selected subgroups or alternative revascularization strategies, but much of it is indirect or not directly aligned with the claim.
conclusion: The statement is overall more supported than contradicted. The key direct evidence is a randomized trial showing no reduction in perioperative death or myocardial infarction with preoperative coronary revascularization before vascular surgery, whereas the opposing studies are mostly indirect, observational, subgroup-based, or compare one revascularization method against another rather than against usual care alone.

claim: In adults undergoing major open vascular, endovascular, or hybrid vascular surgery, preoperative coronary revascularization added to usual care appears to have little or no effect on perioperative or long-term all-cause mortality compared with usual care alone, although the evidence is very uncertain.
support_summary: The strongest direct evidence comes from randomized trials showing no clear mortality benefit from prophylactic coronary revascularization before major vascular surgery. In the CARP trial, long-term mortality (22% vs 23%) and 30-day postoperative MI were similar between revascularization and no-revascularization groups, and the authors concluded this strategy could not be recommended for patients with stable symptoms (PMID: 15625331). A CARP subgroup analysis likewise found no reduction in perioperative or long-term mortality in patients with critical limb ischemia or intermittent claudication (PMID: 16765234), and the DECREASE-V pilot also found no improvement in postoperative or long-term outcomes with preoperative revascularization versus best medical therapy (PMID: 19327412). Reviews/meta-analysis generally echo this theme, with a meta-analysis of high-risk noncardiac surgery finding no significant benefit for postoperative mortality, MI, or long-term mortality, though much of that evidence was not limited to vascular surgery and some review articles are indirect rather than primary evidence (PMID: 17766738; PMID: 16879464; PMID: 16917424; PMID: 19419260).
contradict_summary: Contradictory evidence is mainly observational and mixed in quality. Older retrospective studies reported improved 5-year or long-term survival with selective screening and coronary revascularization in selected vascular surgery patients, especially those with moderate-severe reversible ischemia on thallium testing, but these were nonrandomized and vulnerable to selection/confounding biases (PMID: 12835211; PMID: 10512254), and one item is a decision-model/cost-effectiveness analysis rather than direct clinical trial evidence (PMID: 10392675). A recent retrospective cohort reported that patients with prior preoperative coronary revascularization had higher odds of perioperative MACE, which contradicts a simple “no effect” interpretation but does not directly establish higher all-cause mortality due to likely confounding by baseline CAD severity (PMID: 35799557). Another recent report claims improved 5-year survival with ischemia-targeted coronary revascularization plus best medical therapy versus medical therapy alone (PMID: 38518962), but from the abstract this also appears observational and therefore less definitive than the randomized trials.
conclusion: Overall, the statement is more supported than contradicted. This is because the most direct and highest-quality evidence consists of randomized trial data in vascular surgery populations showing little or no effect of preoperative coronary revascularization on perioperative or long-term mortality, whereas the contradictory studies are largely observational, model-based, or potentially confounded.

claim: In adults undergoing major open vascular, endovascular, or hybrid vascular surgery, preoperative coronary revascularization added to usual care may slightly shorten hospital stay compared with usual care alone, but the evidence is limited and low certainty.
support_summary: No non-zero supporting evidence items were available.
contradict_summary: No non-zero contradicting evidence items were available.
conclusion: This statement had only zero-score or missing-score rows in the input file.

claim: In adults undergoing laparoscopic inguinal hernia repair, transabdominal pre-peritoneal repair and totally extraperitoneal repair probably have little to no difference in serious adverse events, hernia recurrence, or chronic pain.
support_summary: Most comparative studies and reviews report broadly similar outcomes between TAPP and TEP for key endpoints relevant to the claim. A randomized trial found chronic groin pain and quality of life were comparable between techniques, despite more early pain after TAPP (PMID: 23389072), and a retrospective cohort similarly found no significant differences in short-term complications, recurrence, or chronic groin pain, though TEP had more conversions (PMID: 41924687). A meta-analysis of randomized studies found no difference in recurrence or long-term neuralgia, although it also reported higher operative morbidity with TAPP, which tempers support for the “little to no difference” claim (PMID: 23768695). Additional observational studies reported no major complications or recurrence over 2 years (PMID: 27604667), similar postoperative outcomes including chronic pain/urinary retention (PMID: 35602856), and similar long-term chronic pain and quality of life with only seroma differing (PMID: 36383888); however, some included items are weaker or only indirectly informative, such as a narrative review (PMID: 39077677), mixed-comparator study including eTEP (PMID: 37187806), descriptive series without a direct head-to-head comparison (PMID: 25170385), a recurrence-case series not designed to compare TAPP vs TEP incidence (PMID: 40944740), and a case report of bowel obstruction after TAPP that is actually discordant with its placement in support (PMID: 8779300).
contradict_summary: One large single-center consecutive case series suggests TEP may have lower recurrence and fewer nerve injuries than the center’s earlier TAPP experience, reporting 6 recurrences in the first 300 TAPP repairs versus 1 in the first 300 TEP repairs, with additional complications in both groups (PMID: 11178763). However, this evidence is limited by its nonrandomized, sequential design and likely learning-curve effects, since TEP was adopted after the initial TAPP experience, making direct causal comparison uncertain. Notably, some evidence categorized as support also contains contradictory signals, including higher operative morbidity with TAPP in a meta-analysis (PMID: 23768695) and a TAPP-related bowel obstruction case report (PMID: 8779300).
conclusion: Overall, the statement is more supported than contradicted. The strongest body of evidence includes a randomized trial, a meta-analysis, and several cohort studies showing similar recurrence and chronic pain, while contradictory evidence is limited and mainly comes from older or methodologically weaker, potentially confounded comparisons, though some studies do suggest somewhat higher morbidity with TAPP.

claim: In adults undergoing laparoscopic inguinal hernia repair, totally extraperitoneal repair may have a higher risk of conversion to another hernia repair method than transabdominal pre-peritoneal repair.
support_summary: Two comparative studies report higher conversion with TEP than TAPP in adult laparoscopic inguinal hernia repair. A randomized comparison of eTEP, standard TEP, and TAPP found that conversion from TEP to TAPP was significantly higher, while other perioperative/postoperative outcomes were largely similar (PMID: 37187806). A retrospective cohort of 100 adults likewise found a significantly higher intraoperative conversion rate in the TEP group versus the TAPP group, with otherwise similar short-term outcomes and safety profiles (PMID: 41924687). A minor caveat is that PMID: 37187806 primarily compares three approaches and the abstract does not provide detailed conversion counts, but its stated finding still aligns with the claim.
contradict_summary: One small single-center retrospective series of single-incision repairs reported no conversions in either TEP or TAPP, so it does not show a higher conversion risk for TEP (PMID: 22776301). However, this is weak contradictory evidence because the sample was very small (15 patients), involved an early-experience single-incision technique rather than standard laparoscopic practice, and the absence of events in both groups may simply reflect limited power rather than equivalence. Thus, this study is better viewed as inconclusive/neutral than strong contradiction.
conclusion: The claim is overall more supported than contradicted. Two comparative studies directly report higher conversion with TEP versus TAPP, whereas the lone opposing study is a very small single-incision series with no conversions in either group and limited ability to refute the association.

claim: In adults undergoing laparoscopic inguinal hernia repair, transabdominal pre-peritoneal repair and totally extraperitoneal repair appear to have little to no difference in postoperative quality of life.
support_summary: Direct evidence from a randomized controlled trial found that both TEP and TAPP improved SF-36 quality of life after surgery, with no postoperative quality-of-life difference between groups; long-term chronic pain and return to normal activity were also similar, although TAPP had more acute pain early after surgery (PMID: 23389072). A retrospective cross-sectional study likewise reported no significant between-group differences in any SF-36 domains or chronic pain in the late postoperative period (PMID: 36383888). Additional supporting studies report similar postoperative recovery, pain, morbidity, or overall outcomes between TEP and TAPP, but these are more indirect for the specific quality-of-life claim because they did not explicitly measure QoL (PMID: 10993614, PMID: 35602856, PMID: 41924687). A systematic review/meta-analysis focused on chronic groin pain rather than QoL and found no functional difference between techniques when pain affected daily activities, so it only indirectly supports the claim (PMID: 35660083). Overall, the main recurring theme is that direct QoL assessments and broader postoperative outcomes generally show little to no clinically meaningful difference between TEP and TAPP.
contradict_summary: One retrospective cohort study reported that TEP was superior to TAPP for operative time, pain score at 1 week, and return to normal activities, while complications and recurrence were similar (PMID: 39691563). However, this study did not directly measure postoperative quality of life, so its contradiction is indirect and mainly reflects short-term recovery differences rather than demonstrated QoL differences. This creates a discrepancy with the direct QoL studies, which found no significant postoperative QoL differences.
conclusion: The claim is overall more supported than contradicted. The strongest evidence includes direct QoL assessments from a randomized trial and an observational study showing no significant postoperative quality-of-life difference between TEP and TAPP, whereas the contradicting evidence is limited to one retrospective study showing short-term recovery advantages for TEP without directly measuring QoL.

claim: In women with early or locally advanced breast cancer without bone metastases, bisphosphonates other than alendronate and pamidronate and denosumab likely increase bone mineral density and reduce fracture risk compared with no treatment or placebo.
support_summary: Supportive evidence is led by a systematic review/meta-analysis of 14 RCTs in postmenopausal women with non-metastatic early breast cancer on aromatase inhibitors, which found improved BMD with zoledronic acid, oral bisphosphonates, and denosumab versus control/placebo; denosumab reduced fractures by 50%, while fracture evidence for zoledronic acid remained uncertain (PMID: 34958816). A review focused on denosumab reports phase III HALT-BC findings showing significant BMD gains and a 50% reduction in clinical fractures in non-metastatic breast cancer patients receiving adjuvant AI therapy (PMID: 35860287). Multiple narrative reviews consistently state that adjuvant zoledronic acid, and to a lesser extent risedronate/ibandronate, prevent cancer treatment-induced bone loss and improve BMD in early/endocrine-responsive breast cancer; some also state low-dose antiresorptives can prevent fracture increases from endocrine therapy, though often without trial-level detail in the abstract (PMID: 20616898, PMID: 21789117, PMID: 21987034, PMID: 30994985, PMID: 31555046). One real-world comparative study showed denosumab improved BMD and reduced major fractures more than alendronate during adjuvant endocrine therapy, but it was not versus placebo/no treatment and therefore only indirectly supports the claim (PMID: 40653165). Overall, the strongest abstract-level support is for increased BMD, with fracture reduction supported most clearly for denosumab and less clearly for bisphosphonates.
contradict_summary: The main contradictory item is an older Cochrane review whose early-breast-cancer conclusions concern bone metastases recurrence and survival, not the claim's outcomes of BMD and fracture risk; it found insufficient evidence for routine adjuvant bisphosphonate use in early breast cancer for recurrence/survival endpoints (PMID: 22336790). This creates a categorization discrepancy: although listed as contradiction, the abstract does not directly show that bisphosphonates or denosumab fail to improve BMD or reduce fractures in non-metastatic early breast cancer. Thus, contradiction to the specific claim is indirect and limited.
conclusion: The statement is overall more supported than contradicted. The best evidence from a meta-analysis and denosumab-focused review supports improved BMD in non-metastatic early breast cancer, with convincing fracture reduction for denosumab, while fracture-risk reduction for bisphosphonates is less certain and the lone contradictory review addresses different endpoints.

claim: In women with early or locally advanced breast cancer without bone metastases, clodronate and ibandronate reduce fracture risk compared with no treatment or placebo.
support_summary: Supportive evidence is limited and mostly indirect. A randomized placebo-controlled trial in women with recurrent/advanced breast cancer without skeletal metastases found fewer skeletal complications overall with clodronate, with favorable effects on vertebral deformities and nonvertebral fractures, although individual fracture events were few (PMID: 8968035). A drug review also states that oral clodronate reduced cumulative skeletal events including fractures in placebo-controlled breast cancer studies, but this summary spans mixed disease settings and is not specific to early or locally advanced non-metastatic disease (PMID: 10495073). The remaining cited reviews discuss bisphosphonates broadly, bone health, or bone metastasis prevention without directly showing that clodronate or ibandronate reduce fractures in the target population, so their relevance is weak (PMID: 27618899, PMID: 30581537, PMID: 9616882).
contradict_summary: The main contradicting evidence is a Cochrane systematic review of 21 randomized studies, which found that in women with advanced breast cancer without clinically evident bone metastases, bisphosphonates did not significantly reduce skeletal events (RR 0.99, 95% CI 0.67-1.47), and in early breast cancer oral clodronate did not show statistically significant reduction in skeletal metastases; the authors concluded that effectiveness in early breast cancer remained an open question (PMID: 16034900). This directly weakens the claim for women without bone metastases, especially because the abstract does not report a clear fracture-risk reduction in that population. There is also a discrepancy within the evidence set because some “supporting” items are broad narrative reviews or mixed-population summaries rather than direct evidence for early or locally advanced non-metastatic breast cancer.
conclusion: Overall, the claim is more contradicted than supported. The strongest evidence is the systematic review showing no significant reduction in skeletal events in women without bone metastases and uncertainty in early breast cancer, whereas the supportive studies are mostly indirect, mixed-population, or not specific to fracture outcomes in the stated population.

claim: In women with early or locally advanced breast cancer without bone metastases, these bone-modifying agents show little to no effect on overall survival compared with no treatment or placebo, but several agents increase adverse events such as osteonecrosis of the jaw and renal impairment.
support_summary: Evidence supporting the claim includes a nonmetastatic breast cancer retrospective cohort showing no significant overall survival benefit with nitrogen-containing bisphosphonates, with adjusted analyses suggesting marginally worse survival (PMID: 21147689), and a Canadian evidence-based guideline stating bone-modifying agents are not recommended as adjuvant therapy in primary breast cancer to improve survival (PMID: 26237149). Multiple reviews/meta-analyses and safety reports indicate increased toxicities with bone-modifying agents versus placebo or no treatment, especially osteonecrosis of the jaw, and note nephrotoxicity/renal dysfunction particularly with bisphosphonates such as zoledronate (PMID: 24236524, PMID: 25759613, PMID: 31217825, PMID: 35933498). Several additional case reports and reviews also document osteonecrosis linked to bisphosphonates or denosumab, although many involve metastatic disease rather than the exact non-bone-metastatic population in the claim, so they are indirect for population applicability (PMID: 17194992, PMID: 19011355, PMID: 28210558, PMID: 37496231). There is also some inconsistency within the supporting set, as one meta-analysis states bisphosphonates improve breast cancer survival while also confirming increased ONJ risk, which conflicts with the claim's survival component despite being grouped as support (PMID: 31217825). Overall, the strongest direct support is for little/no overall survival benefit and for increased jaw/renal adverse events, but much of the toxicity evidence is extrapolated from broader or metastatic settings.
contradict_summary: The main contradictory evidence is a large Cochrane review of 44 RCTs reporting that in early breast cancer, bisphosphonates reduced bone metastases and provided an overall survival benefit overall, especially in postmenopausal women, directly opposing the claim that these agents have little to no effect on survival (PMID: 29082518). A narrative review similarly cites Early Breast Cancer Trialists' Collaborative Group findings that adjuvant bisphosphonates reduce breast cancer mortality in postmenopausal women, although it also acknowledges serious adverse events including osteonecrosis of the jaw and renal impairment, making it only a partial contradiction (PMID: 30874905). For denosumab specifically, a meta-analysis found no overall survival benefit versus placebo in early breast cancer but reported no increase in overall toxicity and no difference in ONJ with the 60-mg every-6-month schedule, contradicting the adverse-event portion of the claim for that regimen (PMID: 37284523). Thus, the contradictory themes are a survival benefit in at least postmenopausal early breast cancer with bisphosphonates and regimen-specific evidence that denosumab may not increase some key toxicities.
conclusion: The evidence is mixed, but the claim is only partially supported overall. Direct evidence supports little or no overall survival benefit for some agents/settings and supports increased adverse events such as osteonecrosis and renal toxicity, yet high-level contradictory evidence indicates adjuvant bisphosphonates can improve survival in early breast cancer, particularly among postmenopausal women, so the 'little to no effect on overall survival' part is not universally true.

claim: In deceased donor kidney transplantation, continuous non-oxygenated hypothermic machine perfusion is superior to static cold storage, reducing delayed graft function and improving graft survival.
support_summary: The strongest supporting evidence comes from randomized and meta-analytic studies showing that hypothermic machine perfusion (HMP) reduces delayed graft function (DGF) versus static cold storage (SCS) in deceased-donor kidney transplantation, including a landmark paired-kidney RCT that also found better 1-year graft survival with machine perfusion (94% vs 90%) (PMID: 19118301). Multiple systematic reviews/meta-analyses of RCTs and broader study sets consistently report lower DGF with HMP, with relative risks/odds ratios around 0.57-0.83, and some also report improved 1- or 3-year graft survival, especially in expanded-criteria, DCD, or other higher-risk donors (PMID: 24339970, PMID: 30282122, PMID: 32031711, PMID: 37373568, PMID: 38637312). Additional individual studies generally support better early graft function and/or lower DGF with HMP, including DCD and high-DGF-risk cohorts, though survival benefits are less consistently demonstrated at the single-study level (PMID: 21037431, PMID: 25871364, PMID: 28035688, PMID: 28573190). Reviews and observational reports broadly echo that HMP is particularly attractive for marginal kidneys and may improve short-term outcomes versus SCS, but many are narrative or incomplete-abstract reports rather than definitive comparative evidence (for example PMID: 19857479, PMID: 31080771, PMID: 40926043). A key nuance is that most abstracts refer to HMP generally and do not always explicitly specify 'continuous non-oxygenated' perfusion, so support for that exact technical variant is somewhat indirect.
contradict_summary: Contradicting evidence is limited and mostly comes from retrospective single-center analyses showing comparable outcomes between HMP and SCS rather than clear inferiority of HMP. One propensity-matched retrospective study found that delayed HMP used after an initial SCS period to prolong cold ischemia time had similar DGF, complications, and 1-year renal function to SCS despite much longer cold ischemia time, arguing safety but not superiority (PMID: 33210745). Another retrospective cohort found HMP lowered DGF only in standard-criteria donors, not expanded-criteria donors, and showed no difference in graft survival (PMID: 23622652). A further single-center study reported comparable DGF and creatinine outcomes between end-ischemic HMP and SCS, though this conflicts somewhat with the authors' own background statement that HMP is associated with superior outcomes, suggesting internal tension and possible confounding by longer cold ischemia in the HMP group (PMID: 36233566). Overall, the contradictory theme is not that HMP is harmful, but that superiority—especially for graft survival—is not uniformly reproduced in smaller observational studies.
conclusion: The statement is overall more supported than contradicted. Evidence from a major randomized trial and multiple meta-analyses consistently supports lower delayed graft function with hypothermic machine perfusion versus static cold storage, while graft-survival benefit is supported by several higher-level analyses but is somewhat less consistent across smaller observational studies; evidence for the exact 'continuous non-oxygenated' formulation is present only indirectly in many abstracts.

claim: In deceased donor kidney transplantation from donors after circulatory death, adding oxygen to continuous hypothermic machine perfusion provides additional benefits over non-oxygenated continuous hypothermic machine perfusion, including better graft survival, kidney function, and acute rejection outcomes.
support_summary: Supportive evidence includes multiple preclinical DCD kidney studies showing that oxygenated hypothermic machine perfusion improved early graft recovery, metabolic preservation, and some longer-term injury markers versus non-oxygenated perfusion. In a porcine DCD transplant model, oxygenated perfusion reduced creatinine peaks, injury markers, fibrosis, and chronic inflammation compared with non-oxygenated perfusion (PMID: 23731682), and another porcine model found better graft recovery with continuous or brief initial oxygenation than with standard non-oxygenated HMP (PMID: 32012434). A rat/allogeneic and DCD model reported that hypothermic oxygenated perfusion reduced immune activation, improved function and survival, and that these benefits were lost with deoxygenated perfusate, directly supporting a possible rejection-related benefit (PMID: 30747849). Human discarded-kidney experiments showed better ATP preservation with oxygenated perfusion (PMID: 29326416), while reviews describe oxygenated HMP as promising and note reports of improved graft function and reduced rejection/graft failure in controlled DCD transplantation, though these are secondary summaries rather than primary comparative data (PMID: 31834008, PMID: 34879976). A key discrepancy is that several cited support items are reviews, technical reports, or non-comparative series, and the COMPARE phase 3 DCD trial abstract is truncated here, limiting what can be directly confirmed from it (PMID: 33220737).
contradict_summary: Contradictory evidence is limited but includes one direct primary study showing no benefit of oxygenation during HMP in a porcine autotransplant model from heart-beating donors, with better creatinine clearance in the non-oxygenated group during the first 2 days and an explicit conclusion that the data do not support active oxygenation in that setting (PMID: 22992766). Another randomized clinical trial found that oxygenated end-HMP after static cold storage did not improve 1-year graft survival, delayed graft function, eGFR, or acute rejection compared with static cold storage alone in expanded-criteria kidneys from brain-dead donors (PMID: 33881456). However, both studies are only partially aligned with the claim because they are not direct comparisons of oxygenated versus non-oxygenated continuous HMP in DCD kidney transplantation: one used heart-beating donor kidneys and the other compared oxygenated end-HMP with static cold storage in DBD kidneys. Thus, the contradiction mainly challenges generalizability of oxygenation benefit rather than directly refuting the exact DCD continuous-HMP claim.
conclusion: Overall, the statement is more supported than contradicted. The strongest directly relevant evidence comes from DCD-focused preclinical comparative studies suggesting better graft recovery and possible immunologic benefit with oxygenated versus non-oxygenated hypothermic perfusion, while the contradictory studies are either in different donor settings or compare against static cold storage rather than non-oxygenated continuous HMP.

claim: In deceased donor kidney transplantation, short-duration end-ischaemic oxygenated hypothermic machine perfusion and one-hour end-ischaemic normothermic machine perfusion do not provide meaningful benefit over static cold storage.
support_summary: One recent meta-analysis of randomized trials reported that while full hypothermic machine perfusion reduces delayed graft function versus static cold storage, short-term oxygenated hypothermic machine perfusion applied after cold storage and normothermic machine perfusion were equivalent to static cold storage for delayed graft function and 1-year graft survival, directly aligning with the claim (PMID: 38637312). This is the only evidence item that specifically matches the claim’s focus on short-duration end-ischaemic oxygenated hypothermic perfusion and short normothermic perfusion.
contradict_summary: Most contradicting items show benefit of machine perfusion over static cold storage, especially for continuous/full hypothermic machine perfusion, with randomized trials and meta-analyses reporting reduced delayed graft function and sometimes improved graft survival (PMID: 19118301, 21037431, 28573190, 30282122, 32031711, 37373568). However, this creates a scope discrepancy because many of these studies evaluate broader or continuous hypothermic machine perfusion rather than the specific interventions in the claim (short-duration end-ischaemic oxygenated HMP or 1-hour end-ischaemic NMP). Several other contradicting items are preclinical, mechanistic, observational, or review-style and suggest possible biologic or early functional advantages of perfusion, but they do not directly establish meaningful clinical benefit for the exact short end-ischaemic strategies in deceased donor transplantation (PMID: 23431486, 25871364, 27556029, 33490378, 34033948, 41799024, 31508984, 39693311, 40926043). Thus, the contradicting literature strongly supports machine perfusion in general, but is less directly applicable to the narrow claim.
conclusion: The statement is overall more supported. Although substantial literature shows that machine perfusion in general can outperform static cold storage, the most directly relevant evidence indicates that short-duration end-ischaemic oxygenated hypothermic perfusion and brief normothermic perfusion do not provide clear meaningful benefit over static cold storage, whereas much of the contradictory evidence addresses different perfusion modalities or nonclinical models.

claim: In kidney transplant recipients, routine universal prophylactic ureteric stenting during graft implantation probably reduces major urological complications, including urine leak and ureteric obstruction, compared with no routine stenting.
support_summary: The strongest supporting evidence comes from repeated meta-analytic/Cochrane reviews of 7 randomized or quasi-randomized trials (1154 patients), which found that universal prophylactic stenting significantly reduced major urological complications overall (RR about 0.24; NNT about 13), including urine leak and ureteric stenosis/obstruction, though these trials were judged low-to-moderate quality and stenting could increase UTI risk unless cotrimoxazole was used (PMID: 16235385, PMID: 16249733, PMID: 23771708). Several comparative single-center studies also reported fewer leaks, stenoses/obstructions, reoperations, hydronephrosis, or major urologic complications with routine stenting versus no routine stenting, including a prospective randomized trial showing fewer leaks/obstructions with stents (PMID: 8709353), retrospective adult cohorts with lower fistula/obstruction rates (PMID: 18409473, PMID: 25498072, PMID: 30056916, PMID: 38284370), and a recent open trial showing fewer urinary leaks though not fewer strictures (PMID: 41022177). Additional case series and reviews describe low complication rates in routinely stented programs and attribute this partly to routine stent use, but these are indirect because they lack a non-stented comparator (PMID: 15686670, PMID: 12826163, PMID: 16182731, PMID: 21693244, PMID: 8411403). A discrepancy within the support set is that some items are opinion/review pieces or uncontrolled series rather than direct comparative evidence, so their probative value is weaker than the randomized/meta-analytic data. Overall, the support side consistently emphasizes reduced early leak and obstruction-type complications, with UTI as the main tradeoff.
contradict_summary: Contradictory evidence includes a randomized trial in low-risk adult recipients that found no statistically significant reduction in leak/obstruction with routine stenting versus selective/no routine stenting (3.5% vs 6.6%; P=0.23) and concluded routine stenting was unnecessary in such patients (PMID: 10972216). A pediatric comparative study also found no reduction in leak or obstruction with prophylactic stents and highlighted migration/removal burdens (PMID: 11260484), while several modern observational studies reported similar major ureteric complication, leak, or stenosis rates with and without routine stenting but higher UTI rates in stented patients (PMID: 30402574, PMID: 41711496, PMID: 32637873). A 2018 systematic review/meta-analysis limited to extravesical ureteroneocystostomy found no significant reduction in urinary leak or obstruction and reported increased UTI risk with stents (PMID: 30577212), directly challenging the older Cochrane findings. Other contradiction items are less direct because they are narrative opinions or single-arm reports showing low complication rates without routine stenting using careful or modified surgical techniques, rather than head-to-head comparisons (PMID: 21116376, PMID: 25013597, PMID: 38597387, PMID: 33428659). Major themes on this side are that modern surgical technique/selective stenting may suffice, benefit may be absent in low-risk or contemporary extravesical settings, and routine stenting may mainly add UTI/stent-related harms.
conclusion: The statement is overall somewhat supported, but the evidence is mixed and context-dependent. Older randomized-trial meta-analyses and several comparative cohorts favor routine prophylactic stenting for reducing leak/obstruction-type complications, yet more recent meta-analytic and large contemporary observational evidence suggests little or no reduction in major urologic complications in modern practice while showing increased UTI risk, so the net conclusion is probable benefit with substantial uncertainty.

claim: In kidney transplant recipients, short-duration prophylactic ureteric stenting still reduces major urological complications compared with no routine stenting.
support_summary: Most supportive studies report fewer major urologic complications—especially urine leaks, ureteral stenosis/obstruction, and related interventions—with prophylactic stenting versus no routine stenting. Stronger evidence includes a randomized/meta-analytic signal showing reduced major urological complications with universal prophylactic stenting (RR about 0.24 in Cochrane reviews, PMID: 16235385; PMID: 16249733; updated review PMID: 23771708), along with several retrospective cohorts reporting lower complication rates in stented groups, including short stent removal at about 2 weeks in some series (PMID: 10953120, PMID: 25498072, PMID: 35769622, PMID: 38284370). Some studies specifically support shorter-duration approaches, such as 2-week removal with marked reduction in complications (PMID: 10953120; PMID: 25498072), while a 5-day external stent protocol showed benefit mainly in living-donor recipients and not clearly in deceased-donor recipients, indicating duration/context may matter (PMID: 19774409). Several additional reviews/case series state that routine JJ stenting appears to reduce early urologic complications, but many are indirect because they lack a non-stented comparator (e.g., PMID: 16182731, PMID: 18261587, PMID: 20030524). A discrepancy within the support set is that some articles also report higher UTI or BK-related risks with stents, so support is stronger for reducing major surgical/urologic complications than for overall complication burden (PMID: 25498072, PMID: 25295178, PMID: 41022177).
contradict_summary: Contradictory evidence includes a randomized trial in low-risk adult recipients finding no significant reduction in leak/obstruction with routine stenting versus selective/no routine stenting (3.5% vs 6.6%, P=0.23; PMID: 10972216), a pediatric comparative study showing no reduction in leak or obstruction and noting stent migration/need for anesthesia for removal (PMID: 11260484), and several observational studies/meta-analyses concluding no clear benefit for routine stenting on major urologic complications (PMID: 30402574, PMID: 32637873, PMID: 30577212, PMID: 41711496). A major recurring theme is that modern surgical technique may achieve low complication rates without routine stenting, as shown in large single-center non-stented series with low urologic complication rates, though these are not direct randomized comparisons and therefore are less definitive for causal inference (PMID: 33428659, PMID: 38597387). Another theme is harm: multiple studies found higher UTI rates with stenting, even when major urologic complication rates were similar (PMID: 30577212, PMID: 41711496), and one opinion/review article argues that sound surgical technique rather than routine stenting is the key determinant of outcomes (PMID: 21116376). The strongest contradiction comes from more contemporary analyses suggesting universal prophylactic stenting may not lower major ureteric complications in current practice, especially with extravesical ureteroneocystostomy or selected recipients (PMID: 30577212, PMID: 41711496). Some contradiction items are weaker because they are expert opinion or uncontrolled series rather than direct stent-versus-no-stent trials (PMID: 21116376, PMID: 33428659, PMID: 38597387).
conclusion: The statement is supported, but only moderately and with important caveats. Earlier randomized/meta-analytic and several cohort studies favor prophylactic stenting—including some short-duration protocols—for reducing major urological complications, but contemporary randomized/large observational evidence and meta-analysis show that this benefit may be absent or attenuated in selected low-risk patients or with modern surgical techniques, while UTI risk may increase.

claim: In kidney transplant recipients, prophylactic ureteric stenting has uncertain effects on urinary tract infection risk and does not appear to increase recurrent or severe haematuria compared with no routine stenting.
support_summary: Several retrospective or prospective comparative studies in kidney transplant recipients found no statistically significant difference in post-transplant UTI rates between routinely stented and non-stented patients, including cohorts from adults and children (PMID: 26022012, PMID: 30402574, PMID: 30777542, PMID: 38284370, PMID: 41022177). These studies generally report similar UTI frequencies across groups, supporting the idea that the effect of prophylactic stenting on UTI risk is uncertain rather than clearly harmful. Evidence on haematuria is sparse in this support set: one pediatric stent series reported only a single hematuria event without a no-stent comparator (PMID: 28621639), so direct support for no increase in recurrent or severe haematuria is limited. Overall, the recurring theme is neutral UTI findings in several center-specific studies, but with little direct abstract-level evidence on haematuria.
contradict_summary: Multiple studies, including randomized evidence, meta-analyses, and large observational cohorts, report higher UTI risk with ureteral stenting, with examples including increased overall or recurrent UTI in stented recipients (PMID: 1105928, PMID: 17509336, PMID: 22179401, PMID: 25498072, PMID: 41711496). Systematic reviews/Cochrane-type analyses found UTIs more common in stented patients overall, though one review noted this excess was not seen with cotrimoxazole prophylaxis, introducing some conditional nuance rather than absolute contradiction (PMID: 16235385, PMID: 16249733, PMID: 23771708, PMID: 30577212). Several contradiction items are weaker for this claim because they do not directly compare stent versus no-stent groups or instead compare early versus late stent removal, which mainly shows longer indwelling time increases UTI/stent-related complications (PMID: 11299101, PMID: 28188678, PMID: 30250768). Direct abstract evidence that stenting increases recurrent or severe haematuria versus no routine stenting is minimal; some narrative/opinion-style pieces assert more hematuria, but this is not well substantiated in comparative abstract data (PMID: 21116376). Thus, the contradiction is driven chiefly by UTI findings, with relatively little solid haematuria-specific evidence.
conclusion: The statement is mixed but overall somewhat more contradicted, because a larger and methodologically stronger body of evidence—especially randomized trials, meta-analyses, and large cohorts—suggests prophylactic stenting can increase UTI risk. However, the haematuria part of the statement remains largely unresolved from these abstracts, and several more recent center-specific studies do report no significant UTI increase, so the total picture is not one-sided.

claim: In adults undergoing cataract surgery, trifocal intraocular lenses and extended depth of focus intraocular lenses appear to provide similar distance visual acuity after surgery.
support_summary: Multiple comparative studies consistently report no significant difference in postoperative distance visual acuity between trifocal and EDOF/extended range of vision IOLs after cataract surgery, including prospective randomized and observational studies (PMID: 30089179, PMID: 29980919, PMID: 32368005, PMID: 32844238, PMID: 35661646). Across these studies, the main recurring pattern is similar distance vision but better near vision with trifocal lenses, while some EDOF lenses show advantages in intermediate vision or contrast sensitivity (PMID: 30089179, PMID: 29980919, PMID: 32368005, PMID: 35661646). A mix-and-match study also found no significant UDVA difference among trifocal and EDOF-related lens combinations, though its design is less directly aligned with the simple head-to-head claim (PMID: 35633381). One supporting item appears weaker or potentially miscategorized because it discusses comparability of VA curve shape and simulation rather than explicitly reporting similar postoperative distance acuity between lens types (PMID: 40069915). Overall, the abstract-level evidence strongly and repeatedly supports similar postoperative distance acuity between trifocal and EDOF lenses.
contradict_summary: The sole contradicting study mainly reports differences in through-focus performance, with trifocal lenses better at near and EDOF better at intermediate distance, while contrast sensitivity and dysphotopsia findings were otherwise similar (PMID: 29651343). However, the abstract does not clearly report worse or different far-distance visual acuity for either lens type, so it is at most an indirect contradiction and may be miscategorized relative to the claim focused on distance visual acuity. Thus, the contradicting evidence is sparse and not directly targeted to the stated outcome.
conclusion: The statement is overall more supported. Several studies directly state that postoperative distance visual acuity is not significantly different between trifocal and EDOF IOLs, whereas the lone contradicting item does not directly show a distance-vision difference and mainly addresses near/intermediate through-focus performance.

claim: In adults undergoing cataract surgery, trifocal intraocular lenses may provide better near visual acuity and greater near spectacle independence than extended depth of focus intraocular lenses.
support_summary: Multiple direct comparative studies consistently report better near visual acuity with trifocal than EDOF IOLs after cataract surgery, while distance vision is usually similar and intermediate vision may favor EDOF; this pattern appears in randomized/prospective and real-world cohorts (PMID: 30089179, PMID: 32844238, PMID: 33568894, PMID: 29980919, PMID: 36233597, PMID: 36380047). Several of these studies also found greater spectacle independence for trifocal lenses, especially for near tasks, with markedly lower near spectacle-free rates in EDOF groups in some cohorts (PMID: 32844238, PMID: 33568894, PMID: 34931539). Three meta-analyses/systematic reviews similarly concluded that trifocal IOLs provide superior uncorrected/corrected near vision and higher near spectacle independence, although EDOF may offer better intermediate vision and sometimes fewer photic phenomena (PMID: 33758333, PMID: 35003788, PMID: 36736751). Some items in the support list are only indirect or weakly relevant because they do not directly compare trifocal with EDOF lenses or only compare trifocal with monofocal/enhanced monofocal lenses, which limits their value for this claim (PMID: 30779492, PMID: 35226984, PMID: 36233798, PMID: 38059085). One included network meta-analysis is somewhat mixed, noting no pairwise difference between trifocal and EDOF for intermediate vision and not clearly establishing superiority for near spectacle independence, so it is only indirect support here (PMID: 36136323).
contradict_summary: Only one contradicting study was provided, and it does not directly test the claim's main outcomes of near visual acuity or near spectacle independence under standard postoperative conditions. Instead, it examined tolerance to induced astigmatism and found better objective visual quality with EDOF than trifocal lenses in the presence of astigmatism, regardless of magnitude or axis (PMID: 39247637). This suggests a possible advantage of EDOF under residual astigmatism conditions, but it does not directly refute that trifocal lenses may provide better near acuity or near spectacle independence in general cataract surgery patients. Therefore, this contradiction is limited and somewhat discrepant with the claim being assessed.
conclusion: The statement is overall more supported. The evidence includes multiple direct comparative studies and several meta-analyses consistently showing better near visual acuity and greater near spectacle independence with trifocal versus EDOF IOLs, whereas the lone contradicting study addresses astigmatism tolerance and objective visual quality rather than the claim's primary near-vision outcomes.

claim: In adults undergoing cataract surgery, trifocal intraocular lenses and extended depth of focus intraocular lenses both cause subjective visual phenomena such as glare and halos, with no meaningful difference detected between lens types.
support_summary: Supportive evidence suggests that both trifocal and EDOF IOLs are associated with dysphotopsias such as halos and glare, and some comparative data found no meaningful between-group difference. An optical bench study reported comparable halo intensities across bifocal, trifocal, and EDOF lenses, although this is not a patient-reported clinical outcome and only indirectly supports the claim (PMID: 27070236). In a real-world bilateral cataract surgery cohort, patient-reported halos, glare, and night-driving difficulty were similar across Symfony EDOF and two trifocal groups, despite worse near vision and lower spectacle independence with Symfony (PMID: 32844238). An overview of systematic reviews also indicates that presbyopia-correcting IOLs are associated with glare/halos, but its wording suggests multifocal/trifocal lenses may incur these effects more clearly than EDOF, making this item only partially aligned with the claim and somewhat discrepant with its supportive categorization (PMID: 40623803).
contradict_summary: Contradictory evidence is mixed in strength but repeatedly suggests lens-type differences in dysphotopsia rather than equivalence. Several comparative clinical or synthesis studies reported more halo/photic phenomena with trifocal or diffractive lenses versus EDOF variants, including a meta-analysis finding halos more frequent with trifocal than hybrid multifocal-EDOF IOLs (PMID: 33758333), a retrospective comparison showing significantly less glare/halo/starburst with MiniWell than other trifocal/EDOF lenses (PMID: 36233597), and a prospective study where a continuous depth-of-focus lens had more significant glare/halo than trifocal PanOptix (PMID: 37990206). One prospective case series found objective dysphotopsia similar between lenses but worse subjective bothersome scores with EDOF, implying a difference in patient experience rather than no difference (PMID: 29651343). Some items in the contradiction set are weakly relevant or miscategorized because they actually report no significant difference in halos/glare between trifocal and EDOF (PMID: 36736751) or compare multifocal classes mainly against monofocal rather than directly against EDOF (PMID: 36136323, PMID: 33371649, PMID: 41092215); meanwhile, an optical bench study found larger/brighter halos with diffractive trifocals than nondiffractive EDOF lenses, supporting a mechanistic difference but not directly measuring subjective symptoms (PMID: 41459668).
conclusion: Overall, the claim is somewhat contradicted. While there is credible evidence that both trifocal and EDOF lenses can cause glare and halos, the literature summarized here includes multiple comparative studies and meta-analyses indicating that the frequency or subjective burden of these phenomena can differ by lens design, so the 'no meaningful difference' part is not consistently supported.

claim: In adults with periodontitis receiving subgingival instrumentation during active treatment, adjunctive antimicrobial photodynamic therapy compared with subgingival instrumentation alone does not show a clear clinically important improvement in clinical periodontal outcomes, and the certainty of evidence is very low.
support_summary: Several randomized clinical studies found that adding antimicrobial photodynamic therapy/light-activated disinfection to subgingival debridement or instrumentation improved outcomes from baseline in both groups but did not produce significant between-group benefits in key clinical measures such as probing depth and clinical attachment level (PMID: 33543381, PMID: 36719506, PMID: 40865715). Reviews and systematic reviews similarly report no significant added clinical benefit of adjunctive aPDT overall, or only isolated/significant findings in a small minority of heterogeneous studies, with methodological limitations preventing firm conclusions (PMID: 34586209, PMID: 34680759). In residual pockets during supportive care, a meta-analysis of PDT trials found nonsignificant effects on PPD reduction and CAL gain at both 3 and 6 months, and judged the evidence insufficient/poor (PMID: 35579234). One included item is only indirectly relevant because it evaluated visible light plus water-filtered infrared-A rather than antimicrobial photodynamic therapy, but it also found no additional clinical benefit over instrumentation alone (PMID: 33802497). Another broad review notes conflicting long-term results and only modest short-term improvements in some aPDT studies, reinforcing uncertainty rather than clear benefit (PMID: 41881516).
contradict_summary: The contradicting evidence is limited and partly indirect. One randomized trial reported that repeated adjunctive aPDT after regenerative periodontal surgery showed more favorable outcomes in very deep pockets, but this was in a surgical regenerative setting rather than the non-surgical subgingival instrumentation context of the claim, and most overall clinical parameters did not differ among groups (PMID: 38992200). A narrative review of recent local adjunctive agents stated that current human trials suggest there may be a clinical benefit to aPDT, but the abstract also emphasizes high variability in protocols and does not provide a quantitative synthesis or clear evidence of clinically important benefit (PMID: 40574010). Thus, the contradiction mainly reflects signals of possible benefit in selected contexts rather than strong evidence against the claim.
conclusion: Overall, the statement is more supported than contradicted. Most directly relevant randomized and review-level evidence indicates no clear clinically important improvement from adjunctive aPDT over subgingival instrumentation alone, while the few contrary signals come from indirect, heterogeneous, or selected settings and do not outweigh the broader uncertainty and limited-effect findings.

claim: In adults with periodontitis receiving subgingival instrumentation during supportive treatment, adjunctive antimicrobial photodynamic therapy compared with subgingival instrumentation alone does not show a clear clinically important improvement in clinical periodontal outcomes, and the certainty of evidence is very low.
support_summary: Several sources directly or indirectly support the claim that adjunctive antimicrobial photodynamic therapy (aPDT) adds no clear clinically important benefit over subgingival instrumentation alone. A systematic review in supportive periodontal care found meta-analysis of 3 PDT studies showed nonsignificant effects on probing depth and attachment gain at 3 and 6 months, and concluded evidence was insufficient/poor (PMID: 35579234). A pilot RCT reported that single-session light-activated disinfection adjunctive to subgingival debridement produced no additional clinical or microbiological benefit versus debridement alone (PMID: 33543381), while a broader review of new nonsurgical approaches similarly stated that adjunctive aPDT had not shown significant added clinical benefit versus debridement alone (PMID: 34586209). Additional evidence notes only isolated or selective improvements with repeated aPDT, but overall limited benefit and inability to draw firm conclusions because of heterogeneity and methodological flaws (PMID: 34680759; PMID: 36719506); a recent trial in residual pockets in adults with Down syndrome also found no intergroup clinical benefit with adjunctive aPDT re-instrumentation (PMID: 40865715). A long-term review described the aPDT literature as conflicted and limited, with only modest short-term improvements in some studies, which is broadly consistent but less directly aligned with the exact supportive-treatment setting (PMID: 41881516).
contradict_summary: The contradictory evidence is limited and only partly applicable to the claim. One RCT found that after regenerative periodontal surgery, repeated adjunctive aPDT showed improved outcomes in very deep pockets, but this was a surgical regenerative setting rather than supportive treatment with subgingival instrumentation alone, so its relevance to the claim is limited despite directionally conflicting findings (PMID: 38992200). A narrative review suggested there may be clinical benefit to aPDT in recent human trials, but as a non-systematic review with high protocol variability and no clear quantification of effect or certainty, it provides weaker contradiction (PMID: 40574010). Overall, the contradiction mainly reflects possible benefits in selected contexts or subsets rather than strong refutation of the claim's specific supportive-care scenario.
conclusion: Overall, the statement is more supported than contradicted. The best-matched evidence, including a systematic review focused on supportive periodontal care and several clinical studies/reviews, consistently finds no clear additional clinically important benefit from adjunctive aPDT and emphasizes low-quality, heterogeneous evidence, whereas the opposing evidence is either indirect, context-mismatched, or methodologically weaker.

claim: No evidence was found to determine the effects of adjunctive antimicrobial photodynamic therapy for peri-implantitis, and evidence for peri-implant mucositis was insufficient to draw conclusions.
support_summary: Two recent narrative/review-style articles support the claim that evidence for adjunctive photodynamic therapy remains insufficient, especially for peri-implant mucositis and broadly for peri-implant diseases. A decision-focused review states that adjunctive therapies such as photodynamic therapy are not currently recommended for peri-implant mucositis because of limited evidence strength (PMID: 38789757). Another article on non-surgical peri-implant disease treatment says laser/photodynamic therapy may be considered as an adjunct in complex peri-implantitis, but explicitly notes that evidence for adjunctive therapy is insufficient (PMID: 41913605). These abstracts support uncertainty, but neither clearly demonstrates that there was literally "no evidence" for peri-implantitis; rather, they indicate limited or insufficient evidence.
contradict_summary: The contradictory set contains multiple clinical studies and systematic reviews indicating that adjunctive aPDT has at least some evidentiary basis, especially for peri-implant mucositis. Randomized or controlled clinical studies reported improved plaque index and/or probing depth with adjunctive aPDT versus mechanical treatment alone in peri-implant mucositis, including smokers and non-diabetic groups (PMID: 28457847, PMID: 32634654), while other mucositis studies found PDT effective or associated with clinically visible benefits, though sometimes not superior to another active laser comparator rather than to no aPDT (PMID: 39750007, PMID: 40822840), and one item is only in vitro so is weaker clinically relevant evidence despite being categorized as contradiction (PMID: 33803736). Several reviews also conclude that aPDT may improve clinical parameters or show promising results in peri-implant diseases, including mucositis and sometimes peri-implantitis, although they repeatedly note heterogeneity, poor study quality, and limited generalizability (PMID: 34197647, PMID: 38278339, PMID: 37799758, PMID: 37623288). Some reviews are mixed rather than fully contradictory, noting modest/unpredictable peri-implantitis outcomes or no clear superiority in peri-implantitis while mucositis appears more promising (PMID: 27833738, PMID: 33487962), which creates an important discrepancy: there is evidence, but its strength for firm conclusions—especially for peri-implantitis—remains limited.
conclusion: Overall, the statement is more contradicted than supported. While some reviews say the evidence is insufficient or too weak for recommendations, the abstract set clearly shows that evidence on adjunctive aPDT does exist—particularly for peri-implant mucositis—and several studies/reviews report beneficial effects, so the strongest part of the claim (that no evidence was found for peri-implantitis and insufficient evidence for mucositis) is overstated.

claim: Vitamin D supplementation during pregnancy compared with placebo or no intervention may reduce the risk of severe postpartum haemorrhage in pregnant women, although the evidence is low certainty and based on limited study data.
support_summary: One Cochrane systematic review of 30 trials found that vitamin D supplementation alone during pregnancy may reduce severe postpartum haemorrhage versus placebo/no intervention, based on a single trial of 1134 women with a reported RR of 0.68 (95% CI 0.51 to 0.91), and explicitly rated this evidence as low certainty (PMID: 31348529). The same review emphasized that maternal adverse-event data were sparse and that no firm conclusions could be drawn overall, which is consistent with the claim's caution about limited study data and low certainty. No clear discrepancy is present in this supporting item.
contradict_summary: There are no contradicting evidence items provided.
conclusion: The statement is overall more supported than contradicted because the available evidence directly matches it: a systematic review reported a possible reduction in severe postpartum haemorrhage with vitamin D supplementation, while also stressing that the finding is low certainty and based on limited data. However, support is only moderate because this outcome was based on a single trial and sparse adverse-event reporting.

claim: Vitamin D supplementation during pregnancy compared with placebo or no intervention may reduce the risk of low birthweight among infants, although the evidence is low certainty and does not rule out no effect or harm.
support_summary: Multiple systematic reviews/meta-analyses of randomized or quasi-randomized trials report that vitamin D alone during pregnancy may reduce low birthweight versus placebo/no intervention, with pooled estimates ranging from borderline significance in earlier reviews to clearer reductions in later updates (e.g., RR 0.48, 95% CI 0.23 to 1.01 in PMID: 22336854; RR 0.40, 95% CI 0.23 to 0.71 in PMID: 22742603; RR 0.40, 95% CI 0.24 to 0.67 in PMID: 26765344; RR 0.55, 95% CI 0.35 to 0.87 in PMID: 31348529). A meta-analysis focused on birth size also found reduced low birthweight risk and higher mean birthweight with supplementation (PMID: 30791641). Some individual or narrative evidence is consistent with benefit, such as higher infant birthweight in a trial of nulliparous women (PMID: 26962509), but several supporting items are indirect or non-comparative for the exact claim, including observational vitamin D status data rather than supplementation (PMID: 31021992) and broad narrative reviews without a direct low-birthweight comparison (PMID: 31867246, PMID: 31956848, PMID: 32075071). Across these reviews, authors repeatedly note trial smallness, heterogeneity, and risk of bias, so the apparent benefit is qualified by limited certainty.
contradict_summary: The only contradicting item is a withdrawn older Cochrane review reporting just two small trials (232 women) with mixed findings: one trial had fewer low-birthweight infants with supplementation, while another found lower birthweights in the supplemented group, leading the authors to conclude there was insufficient evidence overall (PMID: 21328247). This item does not strongly refute the claim so much as emphasize early uncertainty, and its placement in the contradiction group is somewhat discrepant because the abstract itself presents mixed rather than clearly opposing results. Similar early uncertainty is also visible in an older review listed under support, which likewise found one trial favoring supplementation and another favoring control and concluded evidence was insufficient (PMID: 10796185).
conclusion: Overall, the claim is more supported than contradicted. The strongest evidence comes from several systematic reviews/meta-analyses of randomized trials showing a possible to probable reduction in low birthweight with vitamin D alone, but consistent concerns about small trials, heterogeneity, and study quality mean the evidence remains limited and does not fully exclude no effect or harm.

claim: Vitamin D supplementation during pregnancy, either alone or combined with calcium or other vitamins and minerals, has very uncertain effects on pre-eclampsia, gestational diabetes, preterm birth, and other maternal or neonatal outcomes compared with placebo, no intervention, or regimens without vitamin D.
support_summary: Multiple reviews and meta-analyses conclude that although vitamin D supplementation in pregnancy reliably raises maternal 25(OH)D levels, effects on key clinical outcomes remain unclear because trials are small, heterogeneous, and often low certainty or high risk of bias (PMID: 31964667, PMID: 22336854, PMID: 26765344, PMID: 27355803, PMID: 38950419). Higher-level syntheses report no clear benefit or very uncertain evidence for pre-eclampsia, gestational diabetes, preterm birth, stillbirth, low birthweight, cesarean section, and related outcomes in many comparisons, with some isolated signals of benefit offset by inconsistency or imprecision (PMID: 31964667, PMID: 30901325, PMID: 31581312, PMID: 38950419). Several reviews specifically note mixed directions of effect depending on regimen, such as vitamin D alone possibly lowering some risks while vitamin D plus calcium may increase preterm birth, reinforcing uncertainty rather than a consistent benefit (PMID: 26765344, PMID: 27355803). Reviews focused on pre-eclampsia or broader maternal-fetal outcomes similarly state that observational associations do not establish benefit from supplementation and that trials have not shown an independent preventive effect (PMID: 28709403, PMID: 27558700). One item is more narrative and centers on biologic plausibility and associations rather than supplementation effects, so it only weakly supports the claim (PMID: 20403218).
contradict_summary: Several individual trials and some newer reviews report beneficial effects of vitamin D-containing interventions, especially reductions in pre-eclampsia, gestational diabetes, or improved neonatal outcomes, which conflicts with the statement of very uncertain effects (PMID: 25683660, PMID: 26288706, PMID: 28744522, PMID: 38645785). However, many of these positive studies involve multimineral combinations or vitamin D plus calcium rather than isolated vitamin D effects, making attribution to vitamin D alone difficult and partly limiting how strongly they contradict the claim (PMID: 26288706, PMID: 28744522, PMID: 38645785, PMID: 40590014). Some contradiction also comes from a Cochrane review and dose-comparison meta-analysis reporting that vitamin D alone probably reduces pre-eclampsia and gestational diabetes, though those same abstracts still acknowledge uncertainty for other outcomes and call for further high-quality trials, so they are mixed rather than unequivocally contradictory (PMID: 31348529, PMID: 35796578). Other items are weaker contradictions because they emphasize vitamin D sufficiency, observational associations, or improved biochemical status without directly demonstrating clear outcome benefits versus placebo/no intervention (PMID: 26770200, PMID: 35028513, PMID: 39861407). Overall, the contradicting set contains positive signals but also notable discrepancies in intervention type, design quality, and direct relevance to the claim.
conclusion: The statement is overall more supported. The strongest evidence comes from multiple systematic reviews and overviews showing heterogeneous, low-certainty, and often inconsistent effects of prenatal vitamin D supplementation on major maternal and neonatal outcomes, whereas contradictory evidence includes several positive but smaller, mixed, or combination-intervention studies that do not fully overturn the broader uncertainty.

claim: In adults with idiopathic normal pressure hydrocephalus, cerebrospinal fluid shunting probably improves gait speed in the short term compared with no shunting or delayed shunting.
support_summary: Most supporting abstracts report that gait is the symptom most likely to improve after CSF shunting in idiopathic/suspected NPH, with improvements often evident by 3-6 months; examples include gait improving first in 93% after shunt surgery (PMID: 16239882), 83% gait improvement at 6 months in operated patients (PMID: 17290183), and significant improvement in Timed Up and Go/10-m walking outcomes by 3 months (PMID: 23467281; PMID: 27653897; PMID: 35418936; PMID: 37891211). Broader syntheses also describe shunting as generally effective, with a systematic review of 64 studies reporting average overall improvement rates around 71% (PMID: 23975646), though these do not isolate gait speed or randomized comparisons. Several studies specifically use quantitative gait measures or short-term follow-up, supporting short-term mobility benefit after shunting or after CSF drainage that predicts shunt response (PMID: 26775149; PMID: 3751498; PMID: 7062072). However, much of this literature is observational, pre-post, retrospective, or includes mixed NPH populations rather than direct comparisons with no shunting/delayed shunting, so support for the comparative claim is partly indirect. Some listed items are reviews or case reports and therefore lower quality, and one "supporting" item itself notes only a small gait-speed improvement from recent trials rather than strong benefit (PMID: 41741941), indicating some internal tension within the support set.
contradict_summary: The main contradictory evidence is an older Cochrane review that found no randomized controlled trials of shunt placement versus no shunt and therefore concluded there was no evidence to determine effectiveness of shunting for NPH (PMID: 12137677); this reflects absence of evidence at that time rather than evidence of no benefit. The other contradictory item is a single case report in which gait worsened despite ventriculoperitoneal shunting and later improved with Korean medicine/acupuncture (PMID: 39708439), but the abstract itself states shunting is currently the only effective treatment for iNPH, so its placement in the contradiction set is somewhat discrepant and the evidence is anecdotal. Overall, the contradiction themes are lack of high-quality randomized comparative evidence and occasional nonresponse to shunting, not consistent abstract-level evidence that shunting fails to improve gait speed.
conclusion: The statement is overall more supported than contradicted. Numerous observational and pre-post studies consistently report short-term gait improvement after shunting in iNPH, including quantitative walking measures, but direct evidence against no shunting or delayed shunting is limited, so the comparative certainty is moderate rather than definitive.

claim: In adults with idiopathic normal pressure hydrocephalus, cerebrospinal fluid shunting probably reduces disability in the short term compared with no shunting or delayed shunting.
support_summary: Most supporting abstracts are observational studies, case series, reviews, or case reports reporting improvement after shunting in idiopathic/normal-pressure hydrocephalus, especially in gait and activities of daily living. Larger series and reviews report substantial postoperative improvement rates, such as 72% improved gait at 6 months with a Strata valve cohort (PMID: 21039363), 71% average improvement across 64 studies with lower recent mortality/complication rates in a systematic review (PMID: 23975646), and 90% clinical improvement in a prospective elderly iNPH series (PMID: 8832666). Several studies specifically indicate short-term functional gains after shunting, including improved motor tests and ADL by 3 months (PMID: 34235588), improved walking, exercise capacity, strength, and voluntary walking at 3 and 6 months (PMID: 35418936), and significantly improved TUG gait scores at 3, 6, and 12 months (PMID: 37891211). Additional reports suggest benefits across disability domains such as gait, cognition, continence, independence, driving/employment, and long-term care burden (PMID: 21371890, PMID: 34083550, PMID: 3767314), although many do not directly compare shunting with no shunting or delayed shunting and some are only indirect or anecdotal evidence.
